Dreadhunter is kinda ok now with a couple years of early access gone by, but that's all it is, and clearly that's all it is going to be.  It's a bit better if you are the type to want things to scale up to where a lot of your fighting is automatic, but still the price is punching way above what the game offers, or (from watching the project since before early access) what the devs have experience to deliver.  Its content is lush without being different or interesting, leading to samey fights, your build is eventually fancy as you let more upgrades take responsibility for you (also leading to samey fights), but building it is mainly a process of time investment and perseverance.  The level grind is glacial and comically unrewarding.  The gearing is boring, restrictive, and basically sabotages itself and its mechanics.

The enemies are each fairly one-note, boring to fight, and sometimes just really annoying as you get to later enemies.  Much of the time is spent fighting very few enemies in very few ways, which makes missions feel plodding and samey, up until the end battle which is always a neverending swarm of adds and something you're trying to get damage onto across them.  Granted, adds are required for several of the game's core mechanics to work, but this miasma of the game's few enemies does blur the big fights together in their own equally forgettable way.  There's so much forgettable time you need to invest in slow, small upgrades, but somewhere between one and two dozen hours, things start to come together enough that it's a mildly fun kind of forgettable rather than pure slog, as long as you can avoid thinking about how much time it took--and how much more time it will take--to get to whatever you were doing here.

There are a lot of pain points, especially in the builds/mechanics, that you have to just choke down and put out of mind to play, though, depending on the path you choose.

Also, you can't play the higher difficulty until you clear the entire second highest difficulty, and when you move up, you are both locked to that difficulty and the story progress is completely reset, so you have to do everything over again, but it's still the same level as before and drops the same level stuff, just enemy stats are boosted to make it harder than it was, but in no way aligned to where you come out of the previous difficulty.  Games shouldn't waste player time on artificial nonsense like that.  The back half of the campaign on hard was a waste of time, as is at least the front half of nightmare.  ARPG monster level is a well-trod issue, and this is a known poor way to solve it.  No one just wants to retread the "story" over again, especially when it's just a list of procedural missions you run down until it stops, and you have to wade back through valueless content to get to where you want to be only because the devs pretend that makes sense.

That said, I do think this has a market out there.  If you want a lighter top-down shooter where you start weak and slowly get stronger and less nuanced while running basically the same fairly nice looking missions, grinding out the occasional small upgrade, and adjusting your build (which is mostly "avoid the bad options if you can figure them out") when you feel like it rather than getting reset like in a roguelite, this game tries to walk that line, even if it significantly overstates itself on the store page.  It's like an overpriced edgelord comfort shooter, but sales may eventually line it up for you.
It's an interesting concept, and has lots of potential, but this is nowhere near worth $35.

Updated review (comments won't post for me under other comments):

It took a bit over 2 hours (in hard mode, this genre of game is nothing new to me) before I started to have enough ammo mods dropping and have enough skills in my skill tree that were ammunition gain focused to allow me to have a balance where if I played smart I could face the big boss mobs without just dying for the simple fact that melee gets you killed incredibly fast as dodging back and forth in and out to do minimal damage at that stage of gameplay development, well that sucks majorly. 

2 Hours.

I wrote this original review at 76 minutes and was finding it frustrating. 

Your melee/ranged combat combination is not unusual. It's a staple of games across many genres, with the original DOOM as a perfect example. Diablo IV also has many classes where close in melee and ranged attack abilities overlap to allow functioning builds and play styles. So, not at all unusual.

The problem your game has here is that you run out of ammunition early on and then you just get nuked, and it is not fun. 

I have several friends who picked this game up and most of them have less then an hour of gameplay and they are all the sorts of players that will 100% a steam game on achievements before they move on, and without achievements, they will sink dozens of hours into a game before moving on and a year or so later jump back in again. 

Less then an hour of gameplay. Less then 2 for me. 

We could all have refunded the game at this point (I've played a little more so can't but that's beside the point).

What needs to be seriously considered as a change is that early on, you have better ammunition management available - even if it is on a weaker gun with significant ammunition and ammunition gain abilities, there's an imbalanace for early game first several missions gameplay where you run out of ranged weapon ammunition and melee will get you killed well before you find more ammunition to be impactful. 

Bug Report: There's a random bug that when you move near certain parts of walls or maps (I haven't figure the specifics out at this point), the game teleports you to the furthest central south part of a levels map. The first time it happened I figured it was part of the map in question, the second time it was random no enemies on screen and teleported me behind a curved computer desk that no amount of move/dash/shoot/melee-dash/mell could destroy or get me out of and I was forced to exit the mission as a result. the third time I walked out of the start area and teleported to the other side of the map, it teleported me twice from other points in the map to the same location. This is incredibly frustrating as back tracking to clear rooms or unexplored areas and even being midway through that getting teleported back to the southern central tip of the map...so yeah. This is also part of the currently negative review, but the ammunition issues early and being forced to spec into ammunition skill trees first, that's the big issue on the review here, but this bug is needing to be looked at.

I just can't recommend this in it's current state & especially for the current price. Some hitches in performance from time to time. Crashed several times in the 3 hours I played. Also not a whole lot here, Really only 4 ways to play, builds, melee, kinetic, explosions, plasma. All the maps are very similar. The combat is really good!

Game has just enough content to last for 3 hours and not be eligible for refund. The loot variety is really low. There are about 3-4 different armor pieces for each slot, and there are maybe 1-2 pieces that actually add new mechanics to how everything works. You basically only have 3 synergies - plasma, kinetic or missile. Those are your 3 options of armor perks that modify how your guns feel. Every item piece is basically more or less the same, and you see every weapon and armor piece in first hour. Imagine if everything you ever got was normal items. That's this game. There are no unique or any actually interesting loot items that drastically modify how you play. 

Combat is fun but loot is bad so there's no particular reason to keep playing. It's also really expensive. Buy Hades 2 or Death Must Die instead.
Game is fun but very bare bones and not worth the asking price. $15-$20 suits this game in its current state better.
Fun game but not really much to do tbh but that 35 $ price point does not feel like it fits, 10-15$ imo max for this right now.
Generative AI was used to create this game but is not disclosed in the store page.

Definitely Early Access.

Don't expect a full experience if youre buying within even a few months of this review.

However it's a solid base of performance, graphics and mechanics to build something fun on, so I expect this review to change, as long as the devs work on it.

UPDATE 7-14-25

Still definitely early access, enemy variety is mildly stale, melee progression and features are scarce, and the inventory system is still kind of a nightmare that locks you into a playstyle.

However the game feels pretty great, the combat is satisfying and the sounds are mostly how they should be, it finally lives up to what I first thought I'd get when I bought the game.

I hope progression can be made more fun and flexible, with more features for those who want to dig their teeth into melee combat.

The gameplay loop is alright, and the different weapon builds feel meaningful, but it gets a bit repetitive after a few hours of shoot, dodge, shoot, ability, shoot, dodge, etc. In my opinion it throws too much meaningless loot at you. After clearing a level you come back to your ship and have to sort through a dozen pieces of armor and guns, any one of which will give you something like a 1% boost to some stat, and deciding which to sell.
But what really killed it was when I was looking at the character portraits, something was off. Everything looked too clean and there wasn't a consistent style. That's when I realized some of the art in this game was AI-generated. Immediately uninstalled. If you can't hire artists, don't make games.

I wouldn't recommend this to anyone, but I understand the appeal.

If I were to explain the gameplay of this, I would say it's "Counter Strike for people with slow reflexes." The game focuses on a slow, methodical playstyle, and making stupid decisions will send you to the garage in seconds. This explanation may be weird in 15v15 battles, but it makes much more sense in the 7v7 competitive mode, which is way better than random battles.

It's also a game of RNG; pretty much everything important depends on luck—whether you hit the enemy, whether it deals damage, how much of it, and what enemies you will be fighting, since matchmaking doesn't use SBMM. If that doesn't make you mad, the insane pay-to-win economy will. Playing without paying doesn't make any sense unless you want to keep grinding credits instead of playing fun tanks.

This game made me love tanks on its release and would be one of my guilty pleasures, but it makes me too mad to play it in the long run. :(

I am an experienced player with over 14 years of gaming experience. The game has changed significantly in recent years. It has become more casual than before, losing its charm and strategic gameplay, and now resembles an quick action FPS tank game. Artillery has been cut back so much in favor of fast light and medium tanks that I don't even know where this will lead, does not make sense to play heavy or slower tanks. I only play because I'm used to it, but at some point I'll definitely leave. So sad to see these changes.
I played this game for 14 years and I can now say it is completely unrecognizable. The new UI is terrible and very depressing to look at.

This is coming from a pro at the game, with a recent winrate of 68% and 3486 recent WN8 with over 10k battles played.

This game is good if you want to play with friends, but there's a lot of predatory nature's being practiced such as gambling, overcharging, and pay 2 win advantages. It's no longer really historically accurate like it used to be, but it's pretty addicting which is pretty dangerous. 

The game started good, you actually had to learn the good old fashion skill, knowledge, and experience. But once they 2020 came out, things started dying, lots of good players started leaving the game because all the stuff they learned started becoming obsolete with new mechanics like armor flashlight which shows the weaknesses in real time. I understand they wanted to make it more beginner friendly but all they did with that was make a lot of tanks that normally where less played and had an unusual weak spot, VERY hard to play.

The introduction of tanks that require no skill to play such as artillery's stun mechanic, the BZ-176, the Ares line with it's old HE mechanics that broke the game before (let me tell you they removed the old HE mechanics because it would ALWAYS do half the damage on a none pen which people kept abusing) the XM-57, the DZT, the DBV, the cobra, and the MBT-B all of which gives a very unbalanced advantage to the player. They all speed up the progression of the game WAYYY too fast, to the point nobody enjoys the game aside from the people who speeds up the game with the unfair advantage. 

Just to let you know, I know this because i've been on both sides of the table, being the person who's played those unfair tanks and the person fighting against it, so my point is fairly valid and unbiased.

Long story short, don't open your wallet unless you know for a fact you'll play longer than 4 years, play with friends so the game is actually fun and you have dependable teammates, and there's quite a bunch of learning curbs and unfair things that need to be rebalanced.

The way tanks are spotted in this game in combination with the minimap makes it not fun and mostly about staying/moving in fixed positions on every map. If you don't know the routes to take in a map you will get highlighted and visible for all the opponents tanks that will be in preferred positions and you will get punished for it. 

Want to flank? Only way to not be spotted by the enemy team and show up on their minimap is if you're lucky and the enemy made a mistake in not covering one of the camping positions every map have. If they cover them correctly, at best you will only be spotted and most of their team will know, or you will be shot and killed.

The camera being in third perspective enables the player to see behind rocks or what is happening on the other side of a wall, so when the opponent shoots you can see it from a position of no danger then move out and shoot him. 

Every map plays out that all the tanks go to the preferred routes where there is elevation/building cover, know all the positions where only your overpowered hull is visible and can't be damaged unless maybe if you buy premium ammo.

In most positions, if you shoot a shot, you will be spotted by the enemy team and visible on the minimap for most of their team, and then you have to drive back behind elevation or a rock etc and wait for 10 seconds and then you can move back to behind a bush and repeat the process again. 

This game could have been better without the spotting and minimap.

I've been playing for over 14 years, and I've been coming back a bit since the famous 2.0 update, but my opinion is still mixed, if not [b]completely negative[/b].

[h3]Pay To Win “gold” ammunition[/h3]
Some will say that it no longer costs “gold,” but game credits, and has done so for a long time.
It's a huge joke and bad faith.
This ammunition is so much more expensive that in order for a game to be financially profitable, you absolutely need the premium subscription + WoT Plus, and/or regular purchases of credits or gold with real money. For me, that's the exact definition of Pay To Win.
This ammunition completely breaks the damage blocking mechanics, especially for heavy tanks whose armor is almost useless against “gold” ammunition with its insane penetration. (Are we talking about the Maus? What a joke...)

[h3]Terrible Matchmaking (revamped in 2.0)[/h3]
It's a huge joke, even worse than I remember, with games ending in record time between 1 to 5 against 15.
It's just completely ridiculous.
Of course, it still doesn't take player skill into account, so either you get lucky (RNG) or you don't and just waste your time with a team that's less effective than the War Gaming bots they add for the lower tiers when there aren't enough players.
Not to mention that now, in premium tanks, you'll very often encounter +2 levels, whereas they were limited to +1 with the old Match Making. As a reminder, so-called “Premium” tanks are generally weaker than their standard equivalents, their strength being that they generate more experience and, above all, more credits (except for the Premium tanks “offered” by War Gaming, of course).

[h3]Toxic Community[/h3]
Players are still as toxic as ever. I've lost count of the number of insulting messages directed at the team by players who, more often than not, haven't done anything useful in the game! When they're not pushing you out of the bushes for fun, and when you use the complaint system, absolutely nothing happens.
I have to admit that they are very intelligent to have set up a system to make players believe that they can complain about inappropriate behavior, but in reality it probably does nothing at all.

[b]So basically, unless you have an account with more than 200 tanks, etc., move on.[/b]

I do not HATE this game. I think it was worth what I paid to have a bit of fun and I don't have regrets about purchasing it. What is REALLY cool about this game is the sheer amount of monsters and sprites, and fusing said monsters to create your perfect little killing machine with the coolest traits and finding strong synergies yourself.

That being said, the combat doesn't exactly feel thrilling or satisfying once you have your fusion and spells and what not. It's just a lot of the same over and over again- go through the level pressing 'A' on every object, get into a fight and press 'A' until you win. It has the concept and beginnings of what could be an amazingly fun game, fusing your monsters at the core of that. It just wasn't all that fun to actually PLAY once you have your monsters.

I really, REALLY tried to get excited about this game, but the "fusion" the game boasts of is one of your monsters changing colors, and the other just dissapearing. No "Gekko+bird=quetzkotl" more like "Centaur+hell hound=red Centaur." There MIGHT be more to the game than this, but I just couldn't get invested enough to care.

[h3]60 FPS Limit? Yes

Do you recommend this game? No[/h3]

Almost good, but ultimately grindy beyond belief (or anything close to fun) and by a dev with an attitude issue that seems to think they're smarter than they actually are.

The game touts thousands of hours of gameplay, but it's really just repeatedly dragged out for hours by the excessive amount of grinding required for certain mats to advance questlines or progression elements to build your base. 

Sure, you can summon and fuse thousands of monsters or play different classes, but after the first few floors, it's trivial to come up with a team with any class where you'll likely just be holding down auto attack 99.99% of the time in combat, which is the bulk of those thousands of hours. Since enemies are basically just giant hp bags with one gimmick each and each fight is just beating down groups of the same enemies over and over, it gets super repetitive really quickly. You can change the difficulty if you want, but you see no difference in anything other than monster stats (no reward bonus or anything), so why bother?

There's also tons of equipment and spell crafting systems you could try out, but ultimately most of it is all meaningless due to the lack of difficulty minus a few bosses here or there if you're unlucky or just not good at team building. Sure you can get a bunch of spells and upgrade equipment, but when you can just brute force your way through, again, why bother?

Every little meaningless story drip requires clearing 3 levels of procedural dungeons that are littered with pickups. Would be tolerable if there was an auto explore system, but for reasons only they understand, the dev refuses to add one. So have fun walking all over big square maps looking for whatever piles of foozles it wants you to grab next. Oh, and when it decides to drip more story at you, prepare to deal with lots of 'funny' edgelord-style writing as your reward for doing it.

The game touts crossover play, but again for reasons only they understand, the dev has come up with a system with player controlled id codes to 'save' your game across platforms. Basically you put in a sequence of numbers and this is supposed to allow you to recover your game on another platform. However, since they are non unique ID's and not assigned by the game, any other player could input the same code and decide to delete your data when they think they are seeing 'garbage data'. Don't worry though, instead of actually fixing the system the dev just added a big red letter warning saying they aren't responsible for lost data. Feel like losing those hundreds or thousands of hours of gameplay because you wanted cross platform saves? The dev doesn't care and blames you for using their system.

Oh, and if you don't like how they do anything, they'll just ban you from the community forums here. I got banned for daring to say that the dev should post patch notes on here instead of discord only. Their response was to ban me permanently from the community forums and declare they weren't changing because they "didn't want to clog the steam feed with notes 99% of people would never read anyway" as if they have never heard of a sticky post on the forums..

As it is, check it out on heavy discount at best or just go play a decent monster summoning game from a dev without ego issues instead.

This is less of a game than it is a grind for the purpose of grinding. The core idea of this game is really cool in theory: You play a monster tamer that collects a team of 6 monsters to fight other teams of 6 monsters (6v6 at once, this is more Dragon Quest Monsters than Pokemon). Then on top of that, each monster has a special unique passive, some of which synergize this combo. On its own neither of these factors are terribly unique, but the third tentpole of the game is the idea that your character himself has unique passive abilities that you can earn by collecting perk points. The objective here is to ultimately build a self sustaining engine where your character's abilities synergize with your monster abilities, enabling you to break the game by stacking crazy amounts of damage and ripping enemies apart. As an example, I played as a pyromancer that can set enemies on fire, stack burn debuffs and use them for increasingly potent attacks. I was able to eventually put together a combination of abilities that would 1.Burn all of my allies (they heal instead of taking damage from burning) and my enemies. 2. cast a spell that hits every enemy and recasts itself for each burning enemy (of which there are usually 5 or 6) 3. have another monster who automatically attacks any monster that gets hit with a spell. This results in an engine where the game essentially plays itself. That, in itself, is pretty cool. 

The problem is the rest of the game outside of that loop is pretty wretched. The gameplay outside combat is essentially exploring procedurally generated RPG maker quality dungeons, fighting enemies to gain experience and looting random chests off the ground as the game shovels massive amounts of different currencies and various (mostly useless) items into your mostly indifferent mouth. The writing is perhaps some of the worst I've ever seen in a game, where instead of putting effort into writing a story the game decides that the fact that it is poorly written is a joke in and of itself. You get quests with names like "generic RPG fetch quest" and frequently characters will break the fourth wall to remind you how much all of this sucks. But I could handle all of that if there was actually a carrot on the end of the stick to look forward to. The ugly truth of this game is that there is absolutely no point at which you are rewarded for setting up your engine. You don't fight a special monster, you don't get to go into a cool new dungeon where they've been hiding the fun, you just keep grinding so you can keep grinding so you can keep grinding ad infinitum. Eventually, once I got my aforementioned build set up, the combat consisted of me holding A until everything died in every single fight. There was no variation, no thought, just me watching the endless waves of 6 enemies at a time dying to my machine between navigating the world's worst procgen dungeon maps. You could have achieved the same effect with a grey screen that lets you "hold A to  make numbers flash on the screen faster than you can read them!"

Other note: There are several different classes but they are set up so that they all build very slowly so you have to put multiple hours into a class to figure out whether you even like it or not. The good news is that they all result in the same numbers flashing across the screen (although sometimes with different font colors! How fun!) so you don't really have to worry that you're missing out because you chose a Cleric instead of a Hell Knight or whatever.

I do not HATE this game. I think it was worth what I paid to have a bit of fun and I don't have regrets about purchasing it. What is REALLY cool about this game is the sheer amount of monsters and sprites, and fusing said monsters to create your perfect little killing machine with the coolest traits and finding strong synergies yourself.

That being said, the combat doesn't exactly feel thrilling or satisfying once you have your fusion and spells and what not. It's just a lot of the same over and over again- go through the level pressing 'A' on every object, get into a fight and press 'A' until you win. It has the concept and beginnings of what could be an amazingly fun game, fusing your monsters at the core of that. It just wasn't all that fun to actually PLAY once you have your monsters.

The game is a lot of really cool ideas poorly knit together that the community tries to ignore by abusing a handful of accidental overpowered cheese strategies. Consider it a warning when cheese strategies that other games would consider funny possibilities for repeat playthroughs or flat out patched out by devs are often the expected gameplay if you want to avoid 100+ level grinding. 

For example even in the early game, effects like mass curse will 1-shot your monsters while doing nothing to the enemy, unless you use a 1-shot cheese strat instead of playing the game.

Also, man. The writing sucks so bad. Between the really ugly UI, the low graphics, and the writing, its hard to stick around for so much obnoxious grinding and hard-wall luck-based mechanics to look for the gold nuggets. I could maybe ignore these issues if the gameplay was solid, but they only make the rough parts worse. 

I tried really hard to enjoy this game, because I think it has some great ideas in here. But it is just not worth the slog.

I love this game, but I absolutely hate the image gen/AI gen portraits. The style doesn't fit at all, they look completely out of place with the rest of the style. I wish the dev would replace those. I would prefer no portraits at all over these :(

Difficult to interpret display, uninformative equipment decisions, and just plain boring.

The single biggest issue is the display. You're flying around in space, with hundreds of purely decorative small rocks in making a very crowded background, and it's very difficult to tell the difference between an actual rock you can pick or collide with and one of those background rocks. Only the larger rocks are obviously "real" (meaning they're actually on the same plane as your ship).

It doesn't help that it's a top-down display so you frequently can't see very far in front of your ship. Sometime there are rocks that are a collision hazard that you just can't see because they're off the edge of the map.

There are a ton of upgrades you can buy with your ship - but 90% of the time, it's not clear what they do. Why you'd want one drive over another, or one autopilot over another, for example.

I spent a couple of hours at it, and it was clear that the basic grind was pretty boring, doing the same stuff over and over. Find a rock, blast it, chase down the pieces, repeat.

Launched the game, and 10 seconds in heard the worst sounding AI voice over I have ever heard. Really shows the level of effort and care the developers put into this game. To make it worse, the store page doesn't mention the AI usage anywhere. I highly regret my purchase, and wish I launched it sooner so I could refund.

EDIT: Additionally, I noticed that even the character portraits are all AI generated. This game is entirely false advertising that their assets are their own by not including an AI disclaimer.

So my save file mysteriously disappeared and I like this game but now I'm a little irritated.  I put six hours into this game and this suddenly happened so this might not be loyal to your progress for now.

STAY AWAY. It is frustrating and lazy combat game that ruins what could be a fun, realistic mining sim. It sells itself as "realistic science space mining" but fails to advertise the constant stream of NPC chatter and random attacks that can't be prevented. You are expected to run. Through an asteroid field in a mining ship that has realistic physics. You will drift and wobble before colliding with high speed ice boulders. If the random aggro encounters don't get you, the terrible ship handling and boulders will. This is a frustration engine.

I wanted to love this game and put in a lot of time to grind out the money to buy a good ship with the gear that makes mining not tedious. It took about 20 hours to get roughly 2M credits saved. Ships, crew, and equipment are very expensive and it will take about 1M for a starter ship. The 20 hours put into building a mining ship are wasted because now there people who just show up and attack me. Not pirates, they want nothing except instant chaos. The game stopped being fun when every 30 minutes a new ship sneaks up and start shooting my unarmed mining ship.

This is an sim-lite space combat game with invisible lasers and a constant parade of people in a part of space that should be empty. It has realistic science but unrealistic everything else - the economy is steady and uneventful, the weapons are boring, the NPCs useless, and the area is packed with people. It is simply not worth the time.

Not sure where the development for this has been going, other than that who ever is leading it is doing so into the drain.
I'm not sure how Apocolypse mode finishes the core game, or alleviates the issues with it.
I'm not sure how a visual rework of Duchy (The worst faction) helps.

The game currently feels like an idle kingdom management sim. Multiplayer is pretty boring because it's stale and most of the factions arn't properly fleshed out. Nord and Duchy being the worst cases of this, yet the Asian, Middle Eastern, and Aztec factions are fully fleshed out with custom skins and actual bonuses and negatives. Nord has 2 special trade resources, Duchy doesn't get anything other than a research bonus that is pointless.
Aztec, Asian, and Middle Eastern all have 6+ special resources and special mechanics for them.

It just feels kinda weird. The game needs alot of work, but instead of doing what needs to be done, or expanding on things and adding more content, they rework the entire game for a new difficulty that is demanded only by the yappers in their discord who do nothing but play the game 24/7.
Yea, they got bored of regular mode, stop diverting resources to please 1% of the playerbase and just finish the game and flesh out all the factions, maybe add a few more.
Maybe make combat not be boring.
Maybe make ocean tiles worth more.
MAYBE FINISH THE DAMN GAME!

It's very fun, but it's also designed to be the ultimate time suck, and not in a great way. This is the reason for the non-recommendation. Additionally, the game has a built in limiter to how large your city can become to force you to use outposts, which is just you playing the first half of the game over again at the same time as playing the original. It isn't fun, it's annoying. It's a good game overall, but I cannot recommend this to anyone who is an actual adult. Your time is better spent elsewhere.

I really liked this game playing through it. The atmosphere is great, the puzzles are good, it's not too difficult, it's original and unique.
But! I have one huge problem with this game and it's something I was incredibly disappointed with: the use of AI. AI pictures first appeared just like randomly on walls and stuff and it was small things that you could really easily overlook, but nearing the end of the game, there is a lot of AI generated assets and it really threw me out of the immersion. The devs say it's to "get the uncanny, slightly "off" look of AI-generated content. This was intentional, as it reflects the kind of strange content the Superstructure itself would make" which is an interesting concept but even still that's not an excuse for using AI in your work. I really hope the devs change this and get artists to make the assets for them sometime down the line since plenty of people can really nail the uncanny off-feeling they're looking for. Maybe that will happen down the line since it is in early access but until then, I would not advise spending money on this game if you are against AI.

Having 59 available stats for each hero (=villager), 9 challenge types (fight is one of them) which use different combinations of stats for various real stats (like which stat is health, shield, offence, turn order), and late game expeditions with tens of villagers. Impossible to plan for anything except the fight, and even then you don't care much about each hero details, it's just too much to get through hundreds of stats in total. And the challenges/fight, while simple and boring, requires quite a bit of time to lay out villagers and enemies in order (auto seems to be good only against very weak opponents), use some abilities, and let the fight to resolve by itself. About the worst combat system I have seen which drags the game. The rest of the game is RPG-ish mixed with research & economic development, would had been pretty nice if not the combat.

There might be an interesting game here somewhere behind all the clunk and clutter, but I just don't find any of it fun. The "management" aspect is you putting villagers on duty, then pushing "end turn" until something happens. The "combat" is ... a confusing mess, and, again, just not fun at all. The voice acting is bad to the point that I wish there just weren't any. There's just a lot of clicking in general, and I have no interest in playing for another 20 hours to see if MAYBE there is more to this game than meets the eye. Bad start.

I loved Stellaris, right up until it became clear I was being DLC farmed by Paradox while still waiting for them to fix major base-game issues. My experience went like this: love the game, can’t get enough of it, but can’t actually finish campaigns because of late-game performance problems. A new DLC releases, I buy it, the core issues remain unfixed. The DLC introduces new problems, those aren’t fixed either. Then another DLC releases. Rinse and repeat.

I may have been born in the morning, but I wasn’t born yesterday. There’s only so much rose-colored glasses can excuse, and I hit that limit a year ago. The only reason I hadn’t written a review sooner was I was hoping the performance issues would be fixed then out of pure disgust after they introduced the DLC subscription model.

So no, I do not recommend this game, not because it isn’t fun (it absolutely is), but because certain business practices shouldn’t be encouraged unless you’re comfortable being endlessly farmed for cash.

I would not recommend this title due to the developer/publisher's practices. The game is nearing 10 years old at this point, yet their only concern seems to be releasing overpriced DLC with only minor changes or content additions. The launcher and species editor are designed to highlight missing DLC, relying on FOMO to upsell more content. Updates regularly break community mods and old saves, leading many players suspect this is intentional. Some of the DLC packs where accused of being similar to existing mods that offered a better experience for free. 

The ultimate bundle currently sits at over $300. While game is fun and offers a great experience, the title is dragged down by Paradox's business practices. If you buy the game at base cost ($39.99), It would feel like playing a demo with many of the essential experiences paywalled. I don't think $300 is a reasonable price for the value provided.

The game is very fun, but is running into a large amount of performance issues that cant be overlooked. Modded or vanilla, after a certain point the game chokes to a hold even with a good CPU, GPU and ram because the game runs on a single thread one one CPU core. 

This bottle neck in the design of the engine means that each time the game releases an expansion performance takes a nose dive, if you want mods get ready for the game to slow to a crawl. This is because the game needs to run checks per tick per month for each pop in the game + other empire modifiers. Ever since 2.0 when the player base wanted "bigger number" the game engine simply cant handle it as it was never designed for it. 

Paradox would need to rewrite the engine to use multi threading and other optimization features at a structural level which would be excessively expensive.

This sucks because the game is a absolute blast,  and I love this game to my core, but cant bring myself to play anymore because the performance dive kills me, I dont want to sit there for 5+ minutes for a single month to pass at best, or have my game crash because it cant take the calculation (though that is more rare to be fair).

An ambitious paradox game that fails to model any systems with any depth and ultimately ends up as the shallowest grand strategy game. Despite that, it is still a fun experience, especially if you enjoy sci-fi. However the game has a deep identity problem and cannot figure out which systems it wants to model with how much depth.

Society & Politics is incredibly simplified to the point that ridiculous game play scenarios happen. For example, the game represents all ideology in the form of "ethics" which are a massively oversimplified collection of "the way the society is". Now this, in theory, could allow the player to customise their ideology much more than any other paradox game. Which sounds great! However, it seems the devs were worried that if they made this system too deep, the player would not want to bother. Therefore, there are very few of these "ethics" and they represent radically different ideologies. For example: There is the egalitarian ethic. Does this represent human rights? Does this represent democracy? Does this represent workers' rights? It seems it represents a bit of everything which compresses any political simulation.

Another funny thing is there is an ethic called "spiritualism". Which sort of makes sense, until you remember that empires that share ethics tend to like each other. This implies that a fanatic christian, for example, would like a fanatic muslim because they both like their god a lot?? Very stupid.

There is no demographics or any kind of depth to the economic simulation beyond inputs & outputs for your various buildings. Now, that's not necessarily bad but there is nothing really that the game does with any sort of depth.

Diplomacy & War is also incredibly simplified. Instead of "peace treaties" or "peace conferences" you get a casus belli by either claiming enemy systems or the game just giving you the subjugation casus belli if you are stronger than the enemy. Claiming enemy systems is just spending some currency to get the ability to get them after the war if you occupy them. There is no complex peace terms, reparations, negotiation etc. 

War has a bizarre timer that is "war exhaustion". Now, nearly every paradox game has a feature like this to prevent wars from going on for too long. However here, it is a timer that goes up regardless of whether you are winning or losing. It goes up somewhat faster if you are losing but even if you win every battle, your war exhaustion is going to be almost 1:1 with your enemy's. This means that every war has an arbitrary timer and if you can't beat the enemy within this period, the game forces you to end the war.

Also, on the depth again, occupation and war is incredibly simplified. When you occupy a system, you do not get to control anything. You don't get to even control the star base that you just took over, you do get to use it to repair your ships but that's about it. After occupying a system you'd expect the planets within the system to start starving or getting effected at all from the blockade but nope! Resources magically teleport to planets. Then you might think to bomb the planets but orbital bombardment is incredibly weak! This is especially bizarre because your ships can be equipped with nuclear bombs and yet you will have to bomb a planet for decades before they surrender. You will need to create a ground invasion army and manually invade every planet most of the time. This is very slow and very unrealistic but that's just how stellaris is in general.

In general, the game does not have any system or mechanic that is at all in depth. It tries to be a jack of all trades but feels lacklustre everywhere. You might still have fun but it's also equally likely to leave you frustrated.
$300+ of DLCs and still a beta version product quality, Paradox keeps disappointing with each new update

Stellaris is inspired by Master of Orion 2 and Star Trek.  The planets and star systems look great, but the space battles in Master of Orion 4 look better (just try cinematic mode!).  Stellaris doesn't feel like a space conquest then colonisation game, perhaps a good thing as too much of this can become boring in previous 4x strategy games.  In Stellaris you have to have a Casus Belli i.e. a legal reason for going to war.  Without one, you simply can’t declare war.  This may mean making a Claim on a star system.  

Unfortunately Stellaris feels more of a simulation than a game, but it does give players a number of stories as they play.

Stellaris has an in game encyclopaedia to explain in game concepts, and links to the Paradox wiki for more info.  Though the wiki's comprehensive, it could be more helpful at explaining how to play the game from a beginner's perspective and at giving playing and strategy tips, rather than focusing on tabulating information.  

The DLCs are very overpriced!    

I don't recommend Stellaris because it is too complicated, it's not enjoyable enough to be worth investing the time to fully learn the game.  It does have a basic tutorial (by explaining some concepts as you play), but often tries to teach you how to do things that you can't at the start of the game due to all the rules.  If you really want to buy it, play it on easiest difficulty without any DLCs activated at first as this will remove some of the complexity from the game.  That said the game is still too slow and longwinded (due to being rulebound) and complicated even without the DLCs and this isn't helped by the ongoing updates being made to the game as a lot of the Youtube tutorials are out of date.

JaxRayne has done some very helpful Youtube tutorials for beginners you can play along to.
Paradox's wiki's Beginners Guide is helpful, but needs updating https://stellaris.paradoxwikis.com/Beginner%27s_guide

I'd like to say that Stellaris is one of those games that with some refinement and new content is something I'd highly recommend, but the truth is, this game has been out for years with numerous DLC expansions and it still feels fundamentally broken.

I've sunk a good amount of time into this game (not as much as steam reports, but that's because it's been paused in the background for probably half that time), I digress, the point I'm trying to make is that I've played this game enough to say the designers hit on a nugget of fun and built all the wrong systems around it.

At the core of Stellaris allows for the ability to create a diverse and weird galaxy exploring species. Choosing the origin, government, traits, etc allows the player to developing their own vision of the species lore, backstory and headcannon, which inform all the decisions within the core gameplay loop of confronting and resolving the events and the emergent challenges unique to each playthrough, and it's amazing and is why I've come back exited and then ultimately dissapointed over and over again. 

The problem is that the designers have tried across numerous DLCs to expand on this ethos and most of the time it just results the game become more and more fiddly, and more and more complex. I'll wrap this up with a quick example; in my last play through I'd made it to mid-late game and was dealing with the crisis. Enemy fleets that were way tougher than I expected were rampaging through my system and killing the populations some of my planets. While I was making the tough decisions about where to send my fleets to try and have an impact while avoiding the main deathball of three 100k fleets of the crisis faction, I was constantly bombarded with irrelevant pop-ups demanding my attention. Pop-ups such as leader level-ups, excavation events, dialogs about the hacking I was attempting on another faction, and more. There's way too many systems competing for my attention and after awhile I just start mindlessly dismissing them which erodes the connection I have with the story the game is trying to build and the whole things devolves.

I have had mixed feelings about this game for years and mostly stuck around because sunken cost fallacy. 

The early game is fun as you explore the galaxy find new things and colonise systems and world. Midgame becomes meh were  marauders and some dlc try to keep things fresh with mixed results. End game tends to be tedious because of the amount of planets, stations and fleets you have to keep an eye on and in general there is no point sticking around after defeating the crisis and or fallen empires.

But the big problem is the paradox dlc policy. This sunken cost fallacy as mentioned before has made me stuck around for a long time. But the dlc does not feel worth it for the amount that you pay for it (usually between 10 and 20 euro). Even on sale I find the quality a bit sub-par. Early dlc added entirely new things such as the ascensions perks, megacorps, playing as gestalt empires and megastructures. Current dlc feels more like expensions for these earlier releases. That would have been acceptable were it not for the price tag. Some dlc does add new things but it seems to be very hit or miss. I have played with galactic storms and mostly did not register them unless they left behind a deposit on my planets.
What is very bad is that it seems that some bugs have not been resolved. Losing a game to the synth queen because the rest of the galaxy does not respond to her is not good. Having to manualy start attacking her because the trigger that should have started war with her did not work is even worse. The fact this happens with dlc released over a year ago is what for me broke the camals back.

Another sad side effect of all the dlc and updates is that mods keep being broken. Modmakers have to keep updating their work especially when paradox decides to overhaul the entire game economy or travel system. They have done this 3 times already.

In short the game has an fun start but starts to falter in mid/lategame. There have been attempts to solve this with dlc but the result are mixed and have created a rather pricy dlc policy. I wish I could be more positive but only pick this up if you have money and time to waste and don't mind the occasional jank.

I tried very hard, tried every combination of settings & mods to make this game feel's like a proper PDX game.
Took me some time, but I know realize this game is never gonna match my expectations, juste like every single PDX game since CKII.

This game is an arcade game, I don't expect arcade when playing a PDX product.
It seem's very huge & deep, but it's not.
Whatever your build is, you will 90% of the time play the exact same way, unity rush then win.

Empire strenght is almost only tied to unity, when reaching certain breakpoints your fleet power suddenly skyrocket, everybody becomes afraid of you & won't oppose any resistance.

All that's left after this point is the absurd spam from diplomacy, research, explorers & the war micro-fest.

This game is an RTS, not a 4X.

Stellaris is the kind of game that promises a galaxy and hands you a glorified spreadsheet. The early hours trick you with atmosphere and mystery, but the moment you start actually managing an empire, the illusion collapses. Everything becomes numbers, modifiers, and menus stacked on menus. It stops feeling like space and starts feeling like office work with starfields in the background.
The worst part is how much of the game is carved up into paid expansions. Every major system feels half‑finished until you buy another DLC, and even then it barely holds together. Instead of a grand strategy epic, you get a hollow framework constantly patched, reworked, and resold. The base game feels like a demo for the store page.
Stellaris isn’t a living galaxy. It’s a management chore wrapped in sci‑fi art, endlessly monetized and never truly complete. If you want a universe full of wonder, look elsewhere. If you want to micromanage job slots and resource trickles for hours, this is your game. For everyone else, it’s a galactic disappointment.

I've always hated Paradox and their disgusting DLC practices. I really, genuinely resent them for cornering as much of the 4X market as they have, because I think they make everything they touch worse.

But I also did the math on their "ten dollars a month for infinite DLC access you guys" subscription, because I've never seen that before. For the last 10 DLC releases, it's just under $170 of DLC released over 40 months. So you could pay $400 to get $170 worth of shit, and who knows how much of that shit is even any good? Paradox' shotgun spread approach to DLC means a bunch of it is ALREADY exploitative penny-pinching nonsense; now they're just going to flat-out ask you to give them money for nothing.

Fuck this company, and I will deliberately not purchase anything I see with their name on. I don't care they can write a game that engages a mysterious sixth human sense, cures cancer, and dispenses blowjobs; this company is *trash* and deserves to be regarded as such.

Feels like devs spend more time adding to the hundreds of dollars of DLC this game has, instead of fixing the core experience. The 4.0 update changes a TON, and it's not intuitive on how things work now (not that it was especially intuitive before this, but at least people got used to the quirks). So much stuff feels broken or locked behind DLC.

If you really want a paradox style 4x game in a sci-fi setting, then the base game is probably worth it (on sale).

One of my favourite strategy games that I cannot in good conscience recommend purchasing. 

Changing the balance of the entire game every few updates is their prerogative as the developers; while it deeply frustrates some players, it doesn't bother me so much. 

Adding more content every few months, even though the game is coming up on ten years old, is a gracious amount of care for the playerbase, and while some may find the number of different options to be bloating over time, I appreciate adding expanses of variety to one's expansion into the expanse of space.

But the entire game, with all that they've added, comes to more than $250 american dollars (if you buy the big bundle), and while three hours of playtime per dollar may be a good ratio to some, I have taken a while to get there, and I don't think I can advise anyone else to follow.

Extremely monotonous; even with all the variety of races every game plays out essentially the same.  Build up as fast as you can, build up a fleet as large as possible without bankrupting yourself, get absolutely wrecked at the end by ai "events" with fleets 20x larger than you can possibly build.

Complete waste of time.  Just go back to Master of Orion II if you have a space 4x itch to scratch.

Routine:

Pick-up stellaris for a few weeks to scratch that itch -> Win a few games -> lose a few more -> get a really fun long-term game going -> Paradox updates the game unannounced -> All saves broken due to mechanics being completely flipped on their head -> avoid like the plague for another year or 2 until I forget, yet again, that Paradox is like an toxic-ex that I keep going back to for more and more. 

I preordered this back in 2017, which is when you should have bought it. Don't buy this game now because it WILL break on you and it WILL ruin your immersion into the story of your save games, which has been one of Paradox's whole sticking points for their 4-x Grand Strategy games.

As of now, I would suggest finding a more stable game to play and save yourself the headache of having to debug what random thing went wrong and crashed or froze your gam this time around every playthrough.

It may be a great military sim, I wouldn't know, but I dislike it.

It's a little better if you're doing co-op with someone who's playing the same civilization as you, as you can split up responsibilities and make it a little easier, but even then its still so much to keep track of.

Ah, Paradox. At once purveyors of compelling “grand strategy” sims of almost unique ambition and complexity, and their own worst enemy. Shoveling expensive and maximalist DLCs out for years after release, alongside free updates which may or may not break the game, trash existing saves, and pile system upon spreadsheet-based system until the game resembles an accountant’s migraine, buying a Paradox title is always a gamble. Maybe it’ll work wonderfully, and reward your investment. Maybe it'll be unplayable – with or without some crucial DLC – now, or in the future.

I came to Paradox’s unique oeuvre by way of Europa Universalis IV, and mostly had a blast, even if I’d be considered a novice after sinking merely hundreds of hours into it. I also saw it devolve over the years I dipped in and out of it, as DLC proliferated and, if not all purchased, weird vestigial systems were bolted onto an already overwrought base game. So, I approached Stellaris for the first time in 2025 – nine years after its release in 2016 – with some trepidation. Where would Stellaris land on the Paradox roulette wheel?

In mechanical terms, Stellaris sits somewhere between the simulationist detail of EU IV and a classic 4X in the vein of Civilization. After choosing your starting species – the game will helpfully recommend which DLC to purchase to best enhance the experience, from the 23 options available at time of writing – you’ll set up shop in a corner of galaxy. From there, you’ll grow your empire from the lonely confines of your home system to galaxy-spanning empire. Before long, a clunky diplomacy system, including a time-consuming galactic council, will require you to juggle rivals’ opinions, infiltration levels and voting agendas. It can feel murky rather than Machiavellian. Fleets must be built and shuffled around your empire, though beyond this, there’s little need to worry about logistics. Minerals harvested on the other side of the galaxy can immediately be spent on building an apartment block in your new colony world. Constructing buildings and districts, and allocating different education levels of Pops to them, also demands time-consuming optimisation at higher difficulties. These duties can be turned over to an AI governor, but their decisions can be counterproductive. There’s also a ship editor, where your battle fleets can be customised, but it’s difficult to see what loudouts and tactics work, and why, in the brief pew-pew of fleet engagements. In essence, there are no shortage of gameplay options in Stellaris, but little guidance for the player about what’s important, or what the consequences of a decision might be. Enacting slavery, or ascending to psionic godhood, can feel a lot like reallocating your workforce from energy collectors to mining drills. It’s a lot of busywork, with relatively few interesting decisions to make.

Science fiction can be considered on a sliding scale of “hardness,” with the austerity and intellectual rigor of Arthur C. Clarke and Greg Egan at one end, and the “space fantasy” of Star Wars at the other. One might imagine that Stellaris, as a kind of sci-fi reimagining of Paradox’s Serious Historical Simulators, would join the likes of Alpha Centauri and SOMA at the harder end of the scale. Instead, it makes what seems a conscious effort to be breezy and engaging. Your civilisation starts the game with FTL, your homeworld politically-unified and at peace, and the colonisation of your home system well in hand, with a kind of “no biggie” insouciance. This is not a criticism, as such, but a warning that players looking to recreate The Expanse, or Kim Stanley Robinson’s Mars trilogy, may find their mileage will vary. Stellaris is perhaps tonally closest to Star Trek. Though, if I’m honest, the frequent snippets and micro-narratives would seem most at home in a lesser iteration of that franchise, without the characterisation or sociological insight that elevated the likes of The Next Generation. 

Artistically, Stellaris elides much of the wonder and terror that might come with the inhuman scales of galaxy-spanning empire for cheery accessibility. Solar systems sit in neat, static astrolabes. In the course of a few in-game years – and less real-time than it might take you to pop to the shops for milk – you’ll have surveyed your first neighboring star system, colonized an alien world, and made first contact with aliens. Later additions – Dyson spheres! Star-killing superweapons! – land with a similar uncanny weightlessness. There’s a whiff of box-ticking to some of the features in Stellaris, less about adding mechanical or thematic depth, and more about the designers ensuring every science fiction trope is represented. As in EU IV, some fine incidental sound design helps build atmosphere, but uncanny character portraits and a clutter of icons detract from it. A sub-Vangelis score burbles away in the background: Paradox still desperately need an option to introduce periods of silence between their music tracks.

For all that Europa Universalis looked and played like a world map stapled to fifty Excel spreadsheets, I happily sunk several hundred hours into it, and probably would have kept going had the DLC arms race not rendered the game unplayable. The genius of Paradox’s detailed simulations is in allowing hugely compelling, player-driven stories to emerge from the mechanical murk. In EU IV, the early modern era unfolds over 50-hour campaigns, with the British colonisation of the Americas, France’s wars of conquest in Europe, and the rise and fall of the Spanish empire all arising organically from the game’s systems. Crucially, the player could change the course of history; conquer Spain, as Morocco, say, and leave the Incas to flourish unmolested, or march west from Korea to central Asia, and squash Muscovy before they become the Russian Empire. The simulation would react in fascinating and unpredictable ways. By pureeing down all the quirks and specifics of world history, and extruding it as a sci-fi paste, Stellaris is a less gripping sandbox. The game still offers a rich playground, and gives you commendable freedom to roleplay as alien religious zealots, an AI hivemind, or ultra-capitalist jellyfish. But for me, at least, vanquishing the Ryzongian Hierophants of the Inner Spiral Arm was not as evocative as unifying Italy early and wrestling Napoleonic France to hard-fought defeat. The “crisis” served up toward the endgame is a welcome attempt to reintroduce dynamism and peril during a period many 4X games struggle with. But in arriving from nowhere and almost always taking the form of an invading horde, it again misses an opportunity for gameplay or thematic complexity. What if the player’s actions in the early game came back to haunt them in the endgame crisis? What if difficult decisions had to be made about how best to respond – not just throwing fleets at the invading horde until they’re deleted – and what if Stellaris engaged with those decisions with any seriousness? Works like Iain M. Banks’ Excession have shown how a galaxy-spanning civilisation can be thrown into turmoil by the unexpected, but there’s nothing so subtle to be found here. 

Where, then, does Stellaris land on the Paradox roulette wheel? At time of writing, I found it to be mediocre. A more traditional 4X than its grand strategy predecessors, with many of the rough edges sanded off, and offering some mechanical depth as well as breadth. It’s strongest, for me, during the early game, when suffused with a kind of optimistic, exploratory wonder. Both in terms of gameplay and performance, Stellaris becomes a slug-fest in the late game. After completing one campaign, I felt that I’d seen most of what Stellaris had to offer. If you’re new to Paradox, Crusader Kings III probably exemplifies the best of the studio. Otherwise, Sid Meier’s Alpha Centauri remains a fine 4X with genre-leading writing and worldbuilding.

Suffice it to say that this game is no longer the game I originally paid for. Stellaris has gone through several interations early on, most of which drastically (for the good) changed the overall direction of the game. However with the last major change made a while back (changes to the way planet management and increase in the number of materials (ie addition of things like alloys) I feel the game has become obfuscated for the sake of obfuscation or in order to just prolong it.

You need to build complex networks to get higher grade materials which is made extremely difficult if you didnt not already build your networks with the required balancing of the higher grade materials already in mind. Admittedly this is probably the intent, to give you something to do in the midgame, or something to allow people to min-max early on, for me it feels like many pathways were just closed off and now there are very strict design pathways that need to be followed. This was also evident in other changes like ship balancing, before it felt there were several optimal fleet setups now it feels you always need to have a very balanced loadout. This may be a change for the good in some scenarios like earlier it was possible to just spam torpedo frigates and could easily dominate the AI, this no longer the case, not because AI now builds flak against small ships but rather that the small ships are just useless against larger ones in late game. This definitely improved performance especially in multiplayer where such small ship spam could easily lead to desyncs, I still feel like in a game that appeals to your limitless opportunities, it feels like the game punishes you for not following strict pathways. 

The game has also in typical paradox fashion ben EU4'ified, in that you need a handful of DLCs to actually play the intended full game, otherwise its like playing monopoly without half of the properties.

TLDR Game is complicated and does not feel like it has nice progression, many mechanics are just do this thing because it exists (like panetary management). Roleplay is harder because optimal paths seem way better than all others and if you intend to make it to late game you best follow this strict pathways

I loved Stellaris, right up until it became clear I was being DLC farmed by Paradox while still waiting for them to fix major base-game issues. My experience went like this: love the game, can’t get enough of it, but can’t actually finish campaigns because of late-game performance problems. A new DLC releases, I buy it, the core issues remain unfixed. The DLC introduces new problems, those aren’t fixed either. Then another DLC releases. Rinse and repeat.

I may have been born in the morning, but I wasn’t born yesterday. There’s only so much rose-colored glasses can excuse, and I hit that limit a year ago. The only reason I hadn’t written a review sooner was I was hoping the performance issues would be fixed then out of pure disgust after they introduced the DLC subscription model.

So no, I do not recommend this game, not because it isn’t fun (it absolutely is), but because certain business practices shouldn’t be encouraged unless you’re comfortable being endlessly farmed for cash.

It makes me so incredibly sad to tick the thumbs down for this game. 

For a long, long time, Stellaris was my favourite 4x of all time. The initial release was shaky but had good bones, bones which were reinforced and built upon with some fantastic updates and DLC over the years. The aesthetic was wonderful, the musical score was wonderful, the sheer detail that has been added to allow customisation of your civilisation is incredible, and the variety of events and crises you could encounter meant no two games ever played the same.

But we've long since passed the point where the game should have been called done, and any further changes left for a sequel. We're now into the 3rd round of "patches" which effectively remake core systems of the game from the ground up in order to make it work with the new DLC they keep adding.

I can't say this is my favourite 4x anymore becuase it isn't even the same game that I bought in 2016 anymore. Nor is it the same game I sunk hundreds of hours into during the sweet spot during late patch 2.0 and early patch 3.0. Now we're well into 4.0 and the game is increasingly bloated, broken, and unrecognisable.

So many of these massive, sweeping changes to the core game should have been saved for Stellaris 2, but instead it seems that either good intentions have paved the way to hell, or Paradox simply did their thing and pushed for more DLC and more monetisation, as they always do, and turned the game into a live service mess which it was never designed to be.

Stellaris is one of my favourite games, that I absolutely cannot recommend.
Strarting with the good:
- One of the best Grand Strategy/4X games out there, you can get lost in all the strategy and empire building if that's what you're into, and that's one of the best things about this game
- Great replayability, especially with mods
- Speaking of which, mods. Mods are the absolute CORE of this game for me, things like Gigastructures, ACOT, extended traditions, more planet mods etc. add like 300% of content into Stellaris, which to be fair made the base game simply boring after playing with many of those great mods for so long, which brings me to the bad about the game unfortunatelly

The bad:
- Right now as of stellaris 4.0 and higher the game has changed completely, it seems like the devs just make many core feature of Stellaris from the ground up instead of just updating the game, which for me makes the game unrecognizable, bloated, and forces you to learn basic mechanincs all over again with each patch, which with a game like that takes a looot of time, it's pretty sad in my opinion, it's basically looking at something you love slowly becoming unrecognizable, but each to their own I guess. This also presents a huge issue which is if you want to play on a rolled back version, most great mods are not supported or their legacy versions just don't work, which makes the base game just boring, and the newer versions on the other hand lack many mods that are just broken post updates, because the dev team doesn't care about that either
- 400 $ in DLC is a joke, when you buy the base game you have like 50% of mechanics blocked behind DLCs, and while the new updates seemingly have "free" components, they are made with paid content in mind, which would be reasonable if Paradox wasn't simply greedy with it, this is much more than just making some money off a game that's out for some time it's basically a subscription model for a singleplayer game, and the quantity is disgusting and not at all reflected in quality

Should you buy this game? If you have too much money to spend and don't value your time when your favourite mechanics and mods will inevitably get broken, then sure, otherwise don't do this to yourself, it's sad what became of this great game.

I've enjoyed this game for a long time, but I agree with other reviews saying it should have been called done a long time ago. While the new systems seem okay from the time I have spent with them, it is telling that they feel the need to fully rework the game every few months. Now, I might have overlooked that for a bit longer, but the inclusion of generative AI in any point in development, let alone in the conceptualization of new elements and voice acting, really just serves as a death knell for this game. It demonstrates a supreme laziness in its continued development which is now doomed to only grow worse. If they can't commission a concept artist or have a VA for a few advisor lines, then how can the quality or consistency of anything from here on out be trusted?

In short, a year ago I would have gladly recommended this game. Now, it is becoming little more than a corpse puppeted around by the mediocrity machine. I cannot in good conscience encourage anyone to pick it up at this point.

Stellaris is probably one of my all-time favorite games, but the 4.0 update has really wrecked the game in many aspects. Not that it was perfect before, but it's really at an all-time low.

- Performance - which was 4.0's main promise - is significantly worse than before.
- UI is in the gutter: some of the new screens are plain bad, others are bugged.
- Game is very unstable, prone to crashes, or desyncs in multiplayer, while in my experience it was very stable before.
- The game is missing many QoL features, UX is very bloated. 
- The AI just plain stops working after enough time passes and entirely relies on cheats for any semblance of competence. This creates an unmissable imbalance between the AI being inexplicably strong early game, and inept late game. You need to carefully engineer your game settings between Difficulty, Scaling Difficulty, Difficulty Adjusted AI Modifiers, and the predicted strenght of your own build to have a decent game.
- There is major power creep, little balance to speak of, and old content is never brought up to the standard of the latest updates (when are Necrophages getting fixed?)

These are all well known issues, widely talked about in the community, but Paradox continues to favor releasing new paid content over fixing the game, as they have already ***RELEASED 3 DLCS SINCE 4.0***, leaving a large amount of major issues unaddressed.
 
This one might be personal preference but the main practical change of 4.0, the new districts/buildings system, is really not doing it for me. Especially, stacking special building and their modifiers on a planet or two is *fun*. But now with all the buildings you are required to have on any given planets, and with the reduced total slots numbers, you rarely get the ever have more than one or two unique buil

Not 4.0 related but the portraits they've been adding for a couple years now are for the most part rubbish. The only good batch was the Mammals one that came with BioGenesis. I would not mind if there was a way to disable portraits but there isn't, so you have to deal with the galaxy being filled with low quality meat puppets.

Lastly, I think a lot of people might not relate to all the issues I have, especially people with fewer hours spent on the game. People will still love the game and play it, as will I, because it is great. But to me it is really saddening that even though the game is supported with patches and paid content, Paradox is somehow not making it objectively better over time. What are we paying money for?

TLDR; This is a DLC milking farm, while core problems like end game performance remain a big problem.

The game is very fun, but is running into a large amount of performance issues that cant be overlooked. Modded or vanilla, after a certain point the game chokes to a hold even with a good CPU, GPU and ram because the game runs on a single thread one one CPU core. 

This bottle neck in the design of the engine means that each time the game releases an expansion performance takes a nose dive, if you want mods get ready for the game to slow to a crawl. This is because the game needs to run checks per tick per month for each pop in the game + other empire modifiers. Ever since 2.0 when the player base wanted "bigger number" the game engine simply cant handle it as it was never designed for it. 

Paradox would need to rewrite the engine to use multi threading and other optimization features at a structural level which would be excessively expensive.

This sucks because the game is a absolute blast,  and I love this game to my core, but cant bring myself to play anymore because the performance dive kills me, I dont want to sit there for 5+ minutes for a single month to pass at best, or have my game crash because it cant take the calculation (though that is more rare to be fair).

An ambitious paradox game that fails to model any systems with any depth and ultimately ends up as the shallowest grand strategy game. Despite that, it is still a fun experience, especially if you enjoy sci-fi. However the game has a deep identity problem and cannot figure out which systems it wants to model with how much depth.

Society & Politics is incredibly simplified to the point that ridiculous game play scenarios happen. For example, the game represents all ideology in the form of "ethics" which are a massively oversimplified collection of "the way the society is". Now this, in theory, could allow the player to customise their ideology much more than any other paradox game. Which sounds great! However, it seems the devs were worried that if they made this system too deep, the player would not want to bother. Therefore, there are very few of these "ethics" and they represent radically different ideologies. For example: There is the egalitarian ethic. Does this represent human rights? Does this represent democracy? Does this represent workers' rights? It seems it represents a bit of everything which compresses any political simulation.

Another funny thing is there is an ethic called "spiritualism". Which sort of makes sense, until you remember that empires that share ethics tend to like each other. This implies that a fanatic christian, for example, would like a fanatic muslim because they both like their god a lot?? Very stupid.

There is no demographics or any kind of depth to the economic simulation beyond inputs & outputs for your various buildings. Now, that's not necessarily bad but there is nothing really that the game does with any sort of depth.

Diplomacy & War is also incredibly simplified. Instead of "peace treaties" or "peace conferences" you get a casus belli by either claiming enemy systems or the game just giving you the subjugation casus belli if you are stronger than the enemy. Claiming enemy systems is just spending some currency to get the ability to get them after the war if you occupy them. There is no complex peace terms, reparations, negotiation etc. 

War has a bizarre timer that is "war exhaustion". Now, nearly every paradox game has a feature like this to prevent wars from going on for too long. However here, it is a timer that goes up regardless of whether you are winning or losing. It goes up somewhat faster if you are losing but even if you win every battle, your war exhaustion is going to be almost 1:1 with your enemy's. This means that every war has an arbitrary timer and if you can't beat the enemy within this period, the game forces you to end the war.

Also, on the depth again, occupation and war is incredibly simplified. When you occupy a system, you do not get to control anything. You don't get to even control the star base that you just took over, you do get to use it to repair your ships but that's about it. After occupying a system you'd expect the planets within the system to start starving or getting effected at all from the blockade but nope! Resources magically teleport to planets. Then you might think to bomb the planets but orbital bombardment is incredibly weak! This is especially bizarre because your ships can be equipped with nuclear bombs and yet you will have to bomb a planet for decades before they surrender. You will need to create a ground invasion army and manually invade every planet most of the time. This is very slow and very unrealistic but that's just how stellaris is in general.

In general, the game does not have any system or mechanic that is at all in depth. It tries to be a jack of all trades but feels lacklustre everywhere. You might still have fun but it's also equally likely to leave you frustrated.
