I’ve been playing Lost Ark since the Western beta. At the time, I was working at Amazon in the IT department and wanted to be part of something different outside of my 9–5. I played the game pre-release and post-release, and in the beginning it genuinely felt exciting and fun.

Progressing through the story, exploring the world, and learning the systems was enjoyable—especially because I was playing alongside my best friend. Early on, the game felt rewarding and social, and there was a real sense of momentum as you moved forward.

However, once progression became heavily tied to gear honing, resource grinding, and item level requirements, the experience changed completely. The grind to increase item level became incredibly frustrating and time-consuming, to the point where it felt almost impossible without extreme dedication or spending. This is ultimately what caused my best friend to stop playing altogether.

On top of that, gatekeeping became a major issue. Endgame content increasingly revolves around strict requirements—high item levels, perfect engravings, optimal card sets, and specific classes—making it extremely difficult for returning, casual, or unlucky players to participate. Even when you meet the basic requirements, it’s common to be declined repeatedly from groups unless you vastly overgear the content.

This creates a cycle where:

You need raids to progress

But you can’t get into raids without already being over-progressed

Which pushes players toward burnout, bussing, or quitting entirely

I continued playing for longer—partly due to my connection with the company—but over time it became clear that Lost Ark feels less like a game and more like a second job. Daily chores, time-gated content, and FOMO-driven systems don’t respect the player’s time, especially for anyone with real-life responsibilities.

This sentiment is widely shared in the community. While Lost Ark has excellent combat, beautiful visuals, and some of the best raid designs in the genre, it relies heavily on:

Excessive daily and weekly homework

RNG-based progression that can feel punishing

A gatekeeping-heavy endgame culture that discourages new or returning players

Eventually, burnout becomes inevitable.

Now, I mostly log in only for new content, events, or free rewards, rather than playing consistently. The core gameplay is still impressive, but the overall progression and social systems make it hard to enjoy long-term unless the game becomes your main focus.

Lost Ark is a great game at its core—but one that struggles to balance ambition, accessibility, and community health. If you have unlimited time, you may love it. If not, it can quickly become exhausting.

Lost ark has alot going for it and alot pulling it down.

The good:
The combat has always been good, and with the improvements to supports it was feeling great to play on the surface, secondly where the story doesn't have its peaks like FF14, its still a passable story to enjoy.

The bad: 
1. The "vertical evergreen systems" (yes this is after the removal of trans and elixirs but they were in the game for too long) The game is riddled with over the top in-game vertical systems to 
A. Drain the players of alot of gold
B. Take as long as possible to complete
C. Very heavily layered RNG systems
D. These are per character so if you want to play more than 1 character you are spending weeks/months of gold to catch up
E. Not sunsetting systems for a long time

The systems currently are 
The Card system
This system I would argue is the most new player unfriendly system which takes along time to fill out as any player, each card requires 16 total cards to fill out to finish the card and we have card sets which you need to fill out 6 card slots (in most cases) this system gives you power which is apparent, vital card drops are really rare for what they are and it can take months to a year to fill enough to for players to not have an issue with it.

Honing: RNG based gear upgrades which cost resources which can take along time to accumulate

Karma: Like honing but costs alot of gold for just as much frustration

Advanced honing: Less rng than actual honing but alot slower and somehow more frustrating

Ark Grid: This piece of shit is the newest evergreen system to drop into the game, everything about it rng garbage from the initial cores (which are the sets for this) and the astrogems (which are needed to activate the sets) it has been a universally hated system

2. A developer that seems to have given up/not cared for the product, after this years loaon (lost arks bi yearly trade show), with the content coming it just seems to show they just want to follow the status quo and not innervate/excite the players.

3. The new player experience: Your start to the game is a pretty slow endeavor with the story quests to level 50 before you interface a tiered progression system that is completely outdated, tiers 1-3 content are irrelevant and have 0 players in them so you will be soloing them, but it is very slow to progress through them as a new player as you will not have the resources to bypass them, and you will be there for a while, once you have entered tier 4 what then? well party play will not be accessible as the early content of Tier 4 are currently dead, so you are forced into the solo modes, where yes they are fun for the new player, after some time it will be incredibly boring, once you have caught up to the current raid you are shit out of luck as the game doesn't give you an option to do the raid solo anymore, so if you haven't made friends or joined a discord you're shit out of luck as 99% of the groups will gatekeep you as you do not have a relevant title, with all I have said above you will be going through this hamsterwheel for months-a year to be unable to play at the very end unless you have a supportive group around you to help you

4. Monotonous daily system: which the rewards don't feel worth it, but with the most recent raid they added drops for the current system in the dailies so therefore making dailies feel mandatory again, yes it may have a rested system, but you will be missing out of a third of your weekly resources and therefore missing potential power gains

5. Insidious p2w packages and services: its no lie that lost ark has always been pay to progress but with this current raid schedule the newest packages would give pretty sizeable power gains to people who pay for it, which on its own is pretty shitty, it wasn't something an ordinary person would consider as those packages are usually sitting around 80-100 usd to buy, but the addition of azena's blessing which is a service per character which is about 550 rc per month (which is even more insidious seeing that you can only buy RC at 1000 for 10USD)  which has many tangible rewards which has 0 way to earn with ingame currency, another daily rewards calendar which you pay more RC for more tangible rewards which costs 1250 rc to purchase with another 1500 RC to claim the rewards again, this screams lets milk the players before EOS and it isn't the last nail in this p2w shithole, as finally with one of the more insidious additions, with the newest catchup pass in KR (which maybe is heading to the west on the 17ths patch) they added an additional set of rewards which are far beyond the best rewards that any pass has, the catch? $20+ dollar paywall, all these services over time had made me lose faith in the game and became one of the reasons for quitting

6. AGS's handling of the game start to now, lost ark had an explosive start to it, over time we shouldn't expect to keep said high numbers but their contact with the community at its largest was practically non existant granted it was most likely in the hands of Soo min park, whom was a huge reason why we had alot of kick back on feedback, under his watch the game had flailed down to under 100k peak players, not to mention the controversies, firstly being Pheongate, (quick explanation of pheons, pheons are an additional currency used as a tax on many build defining in game items, which costed 10 Blue crystals to per pheon for 95 was between 4000-7300 gold) During the events of Pheon gate November 2022 ags had handed out a package to each character which had around 15 pheons in them, the players had heralded this as an amazing way to build player trust and love, ags had revealed at the time that it was intended for 1 of those packages per account, now what does ags do here? "this was our mistake sorry enjoy the free pheons and have a wonderful day"? no they had stripped all of the pheons given out, and in the event you had 0 afterwards you were deducted to the negatives, in that event alot of people left the game, it was the first major controversy that dropped the faith in ags and was responsible for alot of my friends leaving the game, though after soo min parks departure ags stepped up and started listening to the players it was too little too late, and now their involvement now is a pointless promotional youtube videos which could have just been a community post.

7. The self-hating community that remains: Every time I had interfaced with the community at large it was honestly a mistake, the people here hate newbies, people with feedback, pretty much people outside of their bubble

I will leave you my few cents regarding lostark, as someone with over 9k hours in the game for the past 3 years, think i can pretty much tell you the reason why you SHOULD and SHOULDN'T play this game at all.

So for the SHOULD:

Are you unemployed or rich? then definitely this might be the game for you, as it currently stands for a ready to raid player, you will be needing either a lot of time to catch up, or spend more than 5K USD on RMT to have your basic stuff ready for it.
Do you enjoy combat with great feel design? then again this game is definitely for you, LostArk biggest strength lies on it's combat feel.

Now for the SHOULDN'T:
If you are someone who is not prepared to be put through long playing time because either there is someone who is messing up a lot with late raids, then yeah expect to lose a lot of time in pulls
If you are someone who gets frustated because the class you picked up is doing poorly, then again you are out of luck, smilegate(the developer) has been pretty bad at balancing older classes vs newly released ones
Are you familiar with WOW fomo systems? then be prepared that every new raid might introduce a new GOLD SINK and a NEW PROGRESSION system
Are you someone who enjoys having to get your feedback ignored? Then be prepared, while AMAZON does a good job at compiling a lot of the feedback, the sad reality is that it gets ignored by SMILEGATE.
Are you someone who wants to min-max? Well that's too bad because SMILEGATE doesn't like it that you can min max your build or char, there is always a need to keep you running on the wheel
You like raiding a lot? Well good news, currently you can do up to 18 raids a week between 6 CHARACTERS
Oh also i forgot, unless you are putting some $$$ into the game, then you must be mindful on your investment into your characters too, hence those 18 raids a week done might be done for you to funnel a character into a hard raid status raid ready while the rest rots in the bottom.

While Lostark has creative raid and combat designs, it is constantly constrained and weighted down by many of the system designs that Smilegate wants to push forward, there is no denying in the potential that the game could have, is still there, locked behind an incompetent developer that listens to whales first rather than the casual player
Yes there are still express events that will let you raise 1 character to a playable status but this won't be enough for you to feel at an okay pace to play the game, my honest opinion is that really there are better things to do, learn a language, workout more, or other useful things, LOSTARK is not a game you want to currently play unless you can ignore all the SHOULDN'T and can be okay with the SHOULD to play it.

So yeah that's it folks, if you play and don't want to stress, make 6 valkyries and go full support or 6 bards too, then you have boobs and not so rough time.

Remember the Fire Phone? Amazon's "revolutionary" device that lasted exactly one generation before being dumped in 2015. Remember Amazon Restaurants? Launched 2015, dead by 2019. Amazon Drive? Gone. AWS DeepComposer, CodeGuru Reviewer, multiple Alexa features? All discontinued in just the last few years.
New World joins a graveyard of Amazon products that were promised long-term support and "commitment to customers" - right up until they weren't profitable enough, fast enough.
Six years for an MMO is pathetic. Star Wars: The Old Republic went F2P when it struggled and is still running 13 years later. Final Fantasy XIV had such a disastrous launch they literally destroyed the world in-game and rebuilt it - and it's now one of the most successful MMOs ever made. Elder Scrolls Online faced massive criticism early on, adapted, and thrives today. Even Fallout 76, which launched as a dumpster fire, is still getting content updates six years later.
These companies fought for their games. They listened. They adapted. Amazon? They're walking away after six years like it's a failed pilot episode.
"Valued community" means nothing when you're just another line item that didn't hit growth targets. We were players, not customers. And the moment the spreadsheet said "not worth it," that was it.
Here's what actually speaks to Amazon: Don't buy their next game. Don't pre-order. Don't give them the benefit of the doubt. They've earned none of it. When a company shows you a pattern - Fire Phone, Amazon Restaurants, Drive, and now New World - believe them. They will abandon you the second it's convenient.
Your time, your money, your community - none of it matters to them as much as quarterly earnings. Act accordingly.

I’ve seen this world grow, fall, and rise again, but we’ve reached a dead end. The lack of fresh content has made the world feel stagnant, and with the news of servers eventually shutting down, there’s no longer a reason to invest time or effort into this journey.

It was an incredible run, and I’ve made unforgettable memories, but I cannot recommend a dying world to new players. Goodbye, Aeternum.

I genuinely don’t understand why games like this aren’t refunded.

This isn’t a free-to-play title that’s shutting down its servers. This is a paid game. I bought it for myself and for my girlfriend. We stopped playing for a while, fully intending to come back later — which is a completely normal thing to do with games you own.

Now we learn the game will be shut down entirely in 2027.

At that point, we’re not talking about a refund policy based on 2 hours played or 14 days owned. Those rules only make sense for games that are not designed to disappear. This game is now guaranteed to become unplayable and has an expiration date, it should not apply.

People paid real money to possess this game, not to rent it unknowingly until Amazon decides to pull the plug. Continuing to sell it without offering refunds or compensation is simply wrong. Honestly, this game shouldn’t even be purchasable anymore given that its shutdown is already planned and announced.

Situations like this are exactly why people say you should never invest time or money into games published by Amazon. Every time, it’s the same story: Amazon does what it does best to take people's money, move on, and leave players with nothing.

This isn’t about entitlement — it’s about basic consumer fairness.

I honestly believe the case with New World must be studied in universities across the world. The game had so much potential, but Amazon was focused on making a game profitable (instead of good) and made so so many wrong decisions over the ~5 year span, totally ignoring their so-called community. It's absolutely mind-boggling, one wrong decision after another, a real example of how NOT to run a game.

Now they are shutting down the servers at the end of 2026. Imagine being as wealthy as they are, masters of cloud storage and not leaving a few servers up and running for the people who invested money and hundreds/thousands of hours in their MMO (Luckily I was not one of them, but how much would it actually cost them?)

If you are planning to play their next Lord of the Rings MMO, which is yet to release - remember what happened to New World and decide for yourself if you should trust a company like Amazon.

I originally got this game on the first day of release. While one friend spent more time in than the rest of use that bought it, we all stopped playing, went back to WoW and completely forgot about New World.  I only have 53 hours of time played in this game even though I've owned it since release day and probably 12 hours of that time has been played in the last couple weeks after the future of the game came to light. 
In short if you didn't already know, it's being removed and the game is effectively dead. There's been mention of the developers of Rust and Garry's Mod wanting to buy it but so far nothing has come from it so if/until that becomes a reality, the game will be no more come January 27th, 2026. This is the reason why I cannot give it a positive review. 
The game itself isn't all that bad. It's engaging, there's lot's to do, combat feels good and the graphics and sound are fantastic. They've made tons of changes and improvements since release day just not enough to redeem themselves like No Man's Sky managed to do years after it's horrible start.

If you're wanting to get it for the achievements, you will have a challenge getting that done in the now under a year left to play unless you play A LOT or the game finds a new owner..
If the game does find a new home, I might come back and revisit the review but for now, I'd have to say just spend your money elsewhere.

So I’m 50/50 on this game, leaning more towards negative. No more content updates aside, this game has a lot of issues. Not just bugs, but core mechanics and gameplay that are awful. Early on before you hit max level it’s really fun. Progressing through the main story, leveling up fast, it’s enjoyable. The main problem stems from after hitting max level. If you’re the type who likes to grind out things solo and doesn’t like PVP, this isn’t for you. Most MMOs I’ve played allowed you to progress or farm for the current best gear solo, even if it took longer. This game doesn’t. And with the player base dying out, queue times to get into things are horrible. PVP is forced on you to upgrade or get better gear. The movement is janky and expect to die because you got stuck in a corner A LOT. Moving back to the content not being released anymore, it causes problems. The player base has dwindled heavily because of it, and it feels like a lot of max level progression relies heavily on having a thriving player base. Which makes grinding your gear score higher and getting the best build take way longer than it should. these are complaints that I’d suggest making a PVP and a PVE option for unlocking gear at max level, and making it so you can either do it solo or with friends. Like many other games, they scale the difficulty with the amount of people participating in said queue. This would ensure that even if the player base died down (like it very much has) someone could still hop on even if he was the only person playing and progress. But, since Amazon has given up on the game, changes like this are never going to happen. So if you don’t like the current state of the game, you’re out of luck because it’s never going to change. How it is now, is how it’ll stay until the servers close. Not to mention the full price tag on it is ridiculous.

I did enjoy my time with this game.  Granted my play time was from before the expansion and then the re-release into Aeternum, so I didn't get the full experience.

But, during my time, this is what I enjoyed:
- Combat.  There were only a few abilities but somehow it was still super enjoyable during the leveling experience 
- Difficulty.  During the leveling experience, the difficulty had enough oomph for a small dopamine hit but not too hard that testing out builds didn't make me feel like I was gimping myself
- graphics and sound: top tier for it's time
- crafting: enjoyable enough that don't really remember anything bad about it and I know I maxed out crafting

Mid:
- story: was there a story? Guess it's just another one of those MMORPG that forgot the RPG aspect. O well
- world: beautiful landscapes and the towns had variety to them.  But too small of a world with not a lot of monster variety 

Cons:
- end-game: you can really tell the devs either pivoted or forgot what their goal was here. Possibly one of the worst end-game experience for a MMORPG. Complete lack of direction. Choose a path and specialize in it, devs!
- company: Amazon is super greedy and it shows in the game.  Telling me to buy Aeternum to play a game I already paid for?  Yeah, f-u
- vision: very similar to end-game. The design philosophy seems a bit all over the place and half baked. Passable or terrible dungeon encounters. Mid to poor pvp. Checklist style content (too similar to retail wow but at least they had a foundation to build upon)

Honestly, I'd give this game a "recommend: yes" review if Amazon Studio didn't do two things.  One, try to charge me again for something I already bought. Two, very bad PR from Amazon as a whole

The character gets even fever and is sick from eating some bananas in a row. It is really weird that I get fever from eating unspoiled stuff and die from starvation although I ate enough. I never had diarrhea from bananas, they are good for your stomach and gut, they give them even to babies if they are sick with digestion problems like diarrhea. Not even completely unripe ones do that. Plums could give you rather a diarrhea. Seriously guys, if I eat in real life what I ate ingame I would have been great!

It can be just very discouraging if your character dies by underweight all the time till you figure out that you need to fill him more than up with food before you go sleeping and then go immideately to bed before he throws up. If you throw up you loose even more weight.

The negative for my review is mainly motivated by that. The game is very nice otherwise and I did struggle my way through for a very long time on countless runs that I lost till I got a hang of it to force-feed.

You don't really have the time to bother about your cooking anyways, because you need to build a sturdy shelter and lots of water reservoirs, a good bed and an oven and craft clothes and tools to survive the upcoming drought. For that reason you need to explore the map as fast as possible to get the resources to research what you need to craft and build. 

Whoever programmed this intended you to die. I like a challenge, but I was angry about the amount of difficulty in this game. Even if you create a custom character with all advantages he will have that trouble.

It feels as if the game punishes you by this the further you are developing everything to just make it impossible to survive. Those mechanics are just torcher for the player to make it for them impossible or nearly impossible to win this game and that makes those mechanics silly and weird. Whoever played other survival games will shake his head about this. There is just an amount of difficulty, a degree of madeup problems that should not be a challenge, because it starts ruining the fun. At least that is what I felt like.

Antibiotics do not help if your fever is caused by food inbalance. Napping the whole day and night, meditating, drinking enough, washing myself and keeping myself indoors for a day or two till I need to get food again did not fight the fever. If you have any wound from climbing or animal attacks you need to wash them directly and then bandage them. 
My mental health was perfectly fine, so that was also not the reason. This happens even if I constantly take antibiotics.
You need to wash the wound itself, if you wash your whole body it won't wash the wound??? Weird.
Not to mention the insane mechanic that some animals will be just killing you or injure you so much, that it's game over for you. I know in tropical areas wounds are not easily healing, but the game makes it often impossible to survive a wound.

The other thing is your psychological status on a long term. You need to work to build your shelter and get the water reservoirs, your clothing and food. Your mental status goes down by that and whatever you try to do it won't go back up. I tried a monkey friend, lots of nice dishes and all the clothing, jewelry, decorations, expanding my home, beautifying it, music and meditations, a shower, no matter what I did, in the end I left the island frustrated and still anorexic. The mechanic they did put together feels wishy-washy, because you just can't get into the clearly positive at your stats, no matter what you do!

All of this would be forgiveable if you had any saves to reload, but you can always only reload the current day you played, or play even more hardcore without any saves.

I really love the game, because the basic idea is great, just the execution...nahhh...just overdone in my opinion. 
For that reason I am not sure if I should recommend it while such mechanics exist. My thumb points here sideways to downwards. On the plus side the game is extremly complex with lots of crafting and recipes. Buy it if you want, but be aware of such weird things to happen.

Give the review a like if it helped you and if you want others to read such infos too.

My first impression was that you die fast by not eating prepared dishes and eating too much veggis or meat or fish. My character was constantly sick from eating or drinking unsave water, which was needed, because it did not rain enough. I did build three water reservoirs, but still only came to day 78, on day 90 the dry time would have been over, but I could not reach there. Customizing my character with a strong immun system and immunized to serious diseases did not help much. I played the game several times through now and died from starvation although my belly was full from eating. 

I got to confess in the beginning I did not really understand how to cook meals. I did build an oven and crafted a cooking pot, but then I could not figure out how to cook meals. It's not that you could just pull anything into the cooking pot in the oven or that the oven shows the recipes. I just cooked then meat, fish and seafood and ate additionally bananas and lemongras and that weakened me till my character was dieing by starvation although he was well fed. 

If that would be realistic then about half of our population would die by being unable to cook, at least some of the bachelors and bachelor girls I do know can only cook some scrambled eggs and coffee at its best :P

A cousins wife in my family could not cook anything tasty, so fastfood it was! They always went out eating. They also did not die by too few vitamins :P

If you eat at real life 30 or 60 days just no complete mixed meals, but single dishes with only fish, meat or veggis you won't get sick and die! This is just extremly silly!

Final Fantasy all the bravest but the rts equivalent of that. 
Not really like... Good?

You're better off with most other rts titles, this game does have it's own charm that I'm sure is for some people. Absolutely wasn't for me though, it's just way too scuffed and sits in my library as I mean to check it out more. 

Like jesus the story is a mess and the description for the setting on the steam page is:

War...
No one knows when or how it started.
In the lands controlled by humans, one fights in the name of peace and justice, one battles for no reason other than to fight, one battles out of greed, to gain more land, and like this, they all faded in the fighting.
The victor edits history to put them in a favorable light, and removes anything that they deem unflattering...
As a result, humanity has lost many things to the wars.
Territory, technology, and their true history...
Now there's not a single country that’s lasted more than 50 years...

Like the literally couldn't be asked to make up shit other than, I guess they're fighting!

I can't really say anything beyond this that wouldn't just be punching down more and saying again how it really wasn't my thing. It does have "something" though, and that special charm is something that I know could be right up the alley of a fair few people I know even. Maybe give this a look, but if you're not sold on some cute png's just smashing together and army composition with experience levels as the max of strategic depth? Probably just move on, there's not really anything for you here.

I can't quite place it but there's something about this game that feels very stiff and lifeless to me. Pizza Tycoon from the 90's was more enjoyable. I'd like to enjoy this game simply because I enjoy coffee, so it would be great if I could play a coffee shop owner while having one, but this is not it and I don't know what would fix it

The game is really nice to play and it has very different activities, like creating your own coffe, ordering, watering plants, repairing furniture. So far I like it, but I do have an issue with using AI for the design of the game. It just feels a little off with the characters looking clumsy and as well the main character being generated with AI. 

Also the hardware optimisation is not the best, getting some lags on high quality with my 5800X3D with an RX9070XT. I do feel the design all in all doesn't align well.

It is a really nice game to spend your time with, and get your coffe business working.
But for me personally the use of AI, especially in Game Art, is a big no, so I give this a negative rating. I hope the developer will be able to create games without AI.

The trailer makes it seem like there's different modes to play, like a zombie invasion, but there's just... not much to do in this game. Fun for a moment, but not much replay value. There is a short campaign, which feels more like a tutorial than a story mode... as well as a sandbox mode where you can play for hours on end (until you get bored). The game feels very basic, unfinished. It would benefit from more of a story, or more strategy elements, or slightly better graphics, more game assets (I wish there was a way to make your own snacks, for example), more in-game content related to the world you're in. For example, I wish there were more ingredients related to Japan, or Australia, etc. How cool would it be to serve a prawn toast with a flat white in Sydney? Or to see local fashions reflect the country you're in? Finally, it would benefit from more locations. It's a coffee game but doesn't include Jakarta or Cuba? Come onnnn.
It's fine as-is, but not worth more than $3 or $4 USD.

Firstly, I want to say that I played this game over 300 hours. I had a lot of fun playing the game, especially in the early stages.
And I enjoyed the game for a lot of hours, I played it on and off since some years.
There were a lot of nice changes and quality of life improvements done, but the last year was just very frustrating and unsatisfying.

I don't understand developers which just ignoring their core audience when people disagree with decisions which a developer plans to do and instead of finding a middle ground and working with the people who are very passioned about the game the developer start to gaslight them with very unrealistic comments for the addressed concerns. 

Lets take this comment for an instance: 
Once again, an event that those with higher levels and heavier equipment will be able to do, while beginners will have difficulty. It's really becoming a fishing planet, while new players with their light rods will have difficulty completing. The event is selective only for those who have already made progress in the game, leaving new players high and dry. Jumping from map to map do the quests, making new players jump from map to spend the few coins they have. But with such poor rewards, I don't think it even matters.

The answer from the Dev:
What's complicated there? All tasks were planned to be comfortably completed by a level 20 player. And the 20th level can be reached in 3-4 days.

End of answer.

https://steamcommunity.com/app/1094780/eventcomments/600782444168261016 bottom of the first page.


The devs say 3-4 Days....this is a fishing game. The dev does not value the time of their customers at all, and you can feel it through the whole game, and even more the higher your level gets.

The XP gain got nuked because there was a hacker problem and in the end it ruined the game because it takes forever now to even get one level from LVL 30+.

Just admit that you want to hard sell your premium to the people for additional XP-boost, so you nuked the XP gain for each fish caught. Even the DLC map fish which had a decent XP gain have gotten nuked to the ground.

The dev even mocked the uproar about price changes in game with an AI generated Beaver when people did not like it.

They announced the price changes here:
https://steamcommunity.com/app/1094780/eventcomments/600780565993500150
 
And put this statement up:

We warned you in advance about the planned changes, so that those who wished to could purchase the equipment in advance. In response, we received nothing but curses. I admit it was a bad experience, and we won't give you any advance warnings in the future.

End of statement.

I mean, sorry that people did not like the changes but did they really have to include a mocking picture in the very next patch notes?

Like yeah, the end of the patch notes were about the price changes, but that picture just feels so provocative and also kinda childish.

The Patch notes:
https://steamcommunity.com/app/1094780/eventcomments/528723641262680541

In the end the devs not only ruined the entry for new players which do not even get hooked because the grind is just way too crazy.
They also have driven away a lot of long term players with their decisions on how they want to go forward with this once very enjoyable game.

The developer ninja-nerfs the already shitty “Christmas event” in the middle of its duration, cutting the rewards by 60% in a typical Russian sly move. People who didn’t sell their “rewards” are fucked. And it’s not the first time the dev has done this shit. If you’re not a masochist trying to get spat on, then avoid this bullshit; it’s not worth your nerves.

the game is good but there are many flaws in it...it's a bit full of annoying bugs but the developer doesn't take you into account if you tell them...I have the impression that if you're not Russian you're not taken into account...an annoying bug when I'm fishing with a raft sometimes I stay for 20 minutes with the rod in the water when I take the rod out of the water the fish is hooked...even though the raft hasn't moved at all...even though the game has been on steam for a few years it doesn't really show that any work has been done on it...even though I like it more than Russian fishing...for now I'm going to leave a negative review...even though there are players on the same server in the game, no one answers you if you have a question, very rarely does a player help you...the tutorial is non-existent...sometimes I wish when I move the mouse through quests it would show me more details about what I have to do and especially about what I win when I complete that quest

game_name: Markus Ritter - Ghosts Of The Past
review_text:
I really appreciate the fan fiction nature of doing another one of these games, and how hard it must have been to make this game. I also really enjoyed the demo which they made, I assume to get funding.

Sometimes, this is quite good, but it also has the worst tropes of games of this kind, clicking on exact spots in exact orders, things not being clickable until the right sequence in the game, obscure puzzles, bad UI etc.

The language keeps defaulting back to German even though I have the settings correct, so I have to reload again and again sometimes. 

I wish I liked this game more, because I really like that this was made.

game_name: Crowalt: Traces of the Lost Colony
review_text:
Crowalt the Traces of the lost colony is just a very long, boring fetch quest galore until 99% of the game is done, and you finally get to know where the Lost Colony is and get hit by a lack luster plot twist.

The thing is, the story is probably the strongest part of the game(along with a pretty decent soundtrack), so the fact that you barely get any story and any lore all throughout the game, in a point and click adventure game with story being it's strongest suit, is just salt upon wounds.

Basically, the story is about how you're a very curious, hyperfocused guy who wants to learn the mystery of the lost colony, you enter the tavern, in an admittedly clever way, stay inside for the night, do mini games to pay off your debt, and then ask merchant for help at repaying said debt, and him helping you find Lost Colony

After you talk with the merchant, the game becomes a huge fetch quest galore, where almost nothing substantial happens! You go help merchant get 1 item, then he tells you to help another guy, and then get this, you spend THE REST OF THE GAME doing fetch quests, just to do that one task for the other guy(there is one touching story about certain two people you get to see but that's about it) Then you get hit with a plot twist, and game ends on a short mini game.

Gameplay isn't hard, which is usually the main issue in p&c adventures, so this game barely has any moon logic puzzles(although if you play it, remember that slingshots are sold by Russ, and use Flint and steel on incense and then use on red tent) but the gameplay issue with Crowalt is, you constantly go around from half across the map, back and forth, back and forth. And the character moves slowly, you have to constantly double tap, wait till he reaches the point you clicked, then double tap, to barely move at reasonable speed.

Despite the fact that merchant gave you the MAP of the settlement and areas around it, you CAN'T use it to just teleport to areas you need. Also the game doesn't have many traditional puzzles that many p&c adventures have, it's just inventory item puzzles and mini games, not sure if it's a negative or positive. I didn't mind it, since usually traditional puzzles in p&c adventures can be long and break pace of the game, but here, the pace of the game is broken by constant fetch quests. 

For example, you'll have to help one guy make one vest, nearby there's another guy who can help you with said task, but he needs a hammer, so you need to go back to the first settlement, then find a trader Russ, do fetch quest for him which takes a while, then you'll go to remove island area to fulfil his task(and you'll have to return a SECOND time here, to learn about the special name for tree forests), to get the hammer, and give it to him, and hopefully you don't forget to click on everything, like I did, to click on a random house before Russ, to do another fetch quest(find 4 fabrics) since you need that woman's help to finish the vest...

The middle of the game is full of this back and forth and it's just not fun. During that time, you don't get story, since your "story" is to do one task for the merchant, who's task is to help another guy. So does the game at least give us the lore of Lost Colony or other stuff? No for the Lost Colony since it's "mysterious" and "no one knows anything"(you only get Lost Colony lore at the very end of the game...) and you do get very very small bits of lore of two settlements you visit, but that's it, and it's pretty underwhelming.

Maybe the characters themselves are interesting to talk to? Well, not really, they barely have any character at all. If I had to describe it, it's like most characters were defined by two/three words, and then Devs forgot to flesh them out. You have main protagonist who's curious adventurer, you have overworked tavern lady, you have mysterious aloof merchant, wise elder, you get the point. Also I get that it's an indie passion project, but not having voice acting in a game with story being it's selling point, is pretty bad.

Lastly, this game, in my opinion, did have a potential to be a good or at least decent game, it's based on a story of the Roanoke colony that mysterious disappeared, it's a pretty good premise for a p&c adventure, but like I said, 99% of the game isn't about solving the mystery of said colony, exploring it. It's just a lot of fetch quests for one guy to give you info, and at the very end he finally gives you said info but it was such a let down. I'm not gonna spoil it, but TL;DR version is that evil magic happened due to hubris of man... That's it, and the plot twist also happens, but jf doesn't really work since it doesn't make you look at the game from different perspective, since, it didn't feel odd to play it.

Worst of all, is that, for it's asking price of 10$, you could've bought better games with more fleshed out stories and gameplay, like Secrets of Monkey Island. 

Heck, there are FREE p&c adventure games on Steam, that you can play right now, that are more fun from story and gameplay perspective:
Metaphobia which has way better story and plot twist, to Alpha Polaris Horror Adventure Game that has better use of limited environments(and has voice acting!), to Knobbly Crook with it's whimsical setting and lore.

So I can't recommend this game to almost anyone, if you got it from some mystery keys pack, and you really hardcore p&c adventure fan, you might get ok 4-5/10 experience from it, but besides that niche scenario, I'd recommend avoiding buying this game.

game_name: Crowalt: Traces of the Lost Colony
review_text:
Person A sends me on a mundane errand leading to person B, who sends me on a mundane errand leading to person C, and so on, and none of these people really have any personality, as if their sole purpose is to send me on errands.

It's quirky and unique and occasionally written with a dry sense of humor, but there are so many better games I could be playing.

game_name: Crowalt: Traces of the Lost Colony
Crowalt: Traces of the Lost Colony
review_text:
Crowalt the Traces of the lost colony is just a very long, boring fetch quest galore until 99% of the game is done, and you finally get to know where the Lost Colony is and get hit by a lack luster plot twist.

The thing is, the story is probably the strongest part of the game(along with a pretty decent soundtrack), so the fact that you barely get any story and any lore all throughout the game, in a point and click adventure game with story being it's strongest suit, is just salt upon wounds.

Basically, the story is about how you're a very curious, hyperfocused guy who wants to learn the mystery of the lost colony, you enter the tavern, in an admittedly clever way, stay inside for the night, do mini games to pay off your debt, and then ask merchant for help at repaying said debt, and him helping you find Lost Colony

After you talk with the merchant, the game becomes a huge fetch quest galore, where almost nothing substantial happens! You go help merchant get 1 item, then he tells you to help another guy, and then get this, you spend THE REST OF THE GAME doing fetch quests, just to do that one task for the other guy(there is one touching story about certain two people you get to see but that's about it) Then you get hit with a plot twist, and game ends on a short mini game.

Gameplay isn't hard, which is usually the main issue in p&c adventures, so this game barely has any moon logic puzzles(although if you play it, remember that slingshots are sold by Russ, and use Flint and steel on incense and then use on red tent) but the gameplay issue with Crowalt is, you constantly go around from half across the map, back and forth, back and forth. And the character moves slowly, you have to constantly double tap, wait till he reaches the point you clicked, then double tap, to barely move at reasonable speed.

Despite the fact that merchant gave you the MAP of the settlement and areas around it, you CAN'T use it to just teleport to areas you need. Also the game doesn't have many traditional puzzles that many p&c adventures have, it's just inventory item puzzles and mini games, not sure if it's a negative or positive. I didn't mind it, since usually traditional puzzles in p&c adventures can be long and break pace of the game, but here, the pace of the game is broken by constant fetch quests. 

For example, you'll have to help one guy make one vest, nearby there's another guy who can help you with said task, but he needs a hammer, so you need to go back to the first settlement, then find a trader Russ, do fetch quest for him which takes a while, then you'll go to remove island area to fulfil his task(and you'll have to return a SECOND time here, to learn about the special name for tree forests), to get the hammer, and give it to him, and hopefully you don't forget to click on everything, like I did, to click on a random house before Russ, to do another fetch quest(find 4 fabrics) since you need that woman's help to finish the vest...

The middle of the game is full of this back and forth and it's just not fun. During that time, you don't get story, since your "story" is to do one task for the merchant, who's task is to help another guy. So does the game at least give us the lore of Lost Colony or other stuff? No for the Lost Colony since it's "mysterious" and "no one knows anything"(you only get Lost Colony lore at the very end of the game...) and you do get very very small bits of lore of two settlements you visit, but that's it, and it's pretty underwhelming.

Maybe the characters themselves are interesting to talk to? Well, not really, they barely have any character at all. If I had to describe it, it's like most characters were defined by two/three words, and then Devs forgot to flesh them out. You have main protagonist who's curious adventurer, you have overworked tavern lady, you have mysterious aloof merchant, wise elder, you get the point. Also I get that it's an indie passion project, but not having voice acting in a game with story being it's selling point, is pretty bad.

Lastly, this game, in my opinion, did have a potential to be a good or at least decent game, it's based on a story of the Roanoke colony that mysterious disappeared, it's a pretty good premise for a p&c adventure, but like I said, 99% of the game isn't about solving the mystery of said colony, exploring it. It's just a lot of fetch quests for one guy to give you info, and at the very end he finally gives you said info but it was such a let down. I'm not gonna spoil it, but TL;DR version is that evil magic happened due to hubris of man... That's it, and the plot twist also happens, but jf doesn't really work since it doesn't make you look at the game from different perspective, since, it didn't feel odd to play it.

Worst of all, is that, for it's asking price of 10$, you could've bought better games with more fleshed out stories and gameplay, like Secrets of Monkey Island. 

Heck, there are FREE p&c adventure games on Steam, that you can play right now, that are more fun from story and gameplay perspective:
Metaphobia which has way better story and plot twist, to Alpha Polaris Horror Adventure Game that has better use of limited environments(and has voice acting!), to Knobbly Crook with it's whimsical setting and lore.

So I can't recommend this game to almost anyone, if you got it from some mystery keys pack, and you really hardcore p&c adventure fan, you might get ok 4-5/10 experience from it, but besides that niche scenario, I'd recommend avoiding buying this game.

review_text:
There has been no meaningful content update in the past 3 years.

The dev also seems to think that 'Hating social interactions' is a good excuse for not sharing any information about the game's development and going radio silent for a year. 

If I knew that's how the game is gonna end up, I wouldn't have bought it.

[h1] If you asked a few years ago, I'd say without a doubt, get this game [/h1]
[h2] But this isn't a few years ago, and, you didn't ask.[/h2]

All the same, and jokes aside, I can't help but just feel disappointed with the team that worked on The Long Drive. Not because the game itself as a foundation is bad; as a foundation and a new type of style for a game, it's fascinatingly fun.
But with every new invention, would come a cascade of bugs. Some would get solved, some would remain, de-sync would forever get worse and inconsistency [b]has been so severe[/b], that nobody could drive in the same car with each-other without the whole thing **exploding** from the inside out.

They haven't finished the game, made an end, and their ambitious world generation updated broke things so bad that it made the game next to unplayable for a while, and, to some extent, it still is.

The response that the community got from the devs, y'know, the same community that funds this team and clearly none of that goes into the product, the team that takes nearly half a decade of slow development and says nothing in return with nothing to show, was 'We hate social interactions, games still being worked on, bye, thanks for patience'.

[i] I get it, life catches up, people got their quirks/the things that make us human. A game is just a game, 'it's whateva'[/i]
Giving people a simple run down of what we can expect out of a product we paid for is a human courtesy. You don't like interaction? Why did you put a game up on steam? Why did you go out of the way to socially put in a consistent amount of time and then assure your community of your commitment to the product, then just bail on it after the hype drops?

Bail might not be the right word, but call it like it is. If you treat your community like a dollar sign and not a supporter of your work, then we WILL turn the other way and never look back, just like they're behaving right now by turning around on the product and giving false assurances so nobody loses hope.

Don't buy this game in its current state of play and with its current state of communication, it's not good. Singleplayer, on certain versions, is very fun (but limited). The rest of the game is broken mechanics, de-sync, lack of communication from the devs, lack of variety and organization, lack of NPC's, broken Multiplayer, and an uncertain future across the board.

Like I said, a few years ago they seemed really into what they were making. I wanted to boot this game up, after so long having left it to dust, to see literally nothing having come, and the petty response of a dev I once respected is the least I could appreciate.
We told __our__ friends, to give YOUR game a shot, put money in YOUR pockets, in hopes of this project coming into a full fold and not just another abandoned state game.

You don't like communicating? Lots of us don't. [b]We do it anyway.[/b] Act more reasonable than that.

The game:
Very fun and weird game with some surprising mechanics (e.g. you can drink from cacti). You've probably seen videos with the weirdness and jank, but to me that's a feature. The car customization is just the right level of complexity. You can play without enemies and hunger/thirst, if you want a relaxing experience. The last beta added online coop (multiplayer) but it's janky like the rest of the game.

The situation:
No updates in over two years (latest beta was in Nov 2024). The dev made a meme post in July 2025 that the game is not abandoned, but since then nothing has followed, so I do not believe that until the game gets an actual update. (AFAIK the game is a one-man project so I guess the dev got burned out since updates used to be frequent)
There's still huge potential here, because a lot of things need to be fixed (bugs and foremost performance!) and basic things like achievements and save file sync (Steam Cloud) are missing. If the game ever gets workshop support, the possibilities are endless.

Verdict:
Buy on deep sale only, unless the dev shows up again.

IN REGARDS TO THE CURRENT TEST BETA:

I know the devs have future plans for the game that no one knows about, but the new world generator is terrible compared to the current public version. I don't quite understand the branching roads and intersections. The new elevated terrain is an atrocity. The main appeal of the public version is being able to just gun it and roll down the road with just enough attention to dodge some rocks in the road. Considering the sheer distance one needs to travel to get to the end, the current beta world generator turns something just engaging enough, into a nightmare slog of slamming on the brakes and figuring out what road to turn down.
I don't understand. Are we expected to travel at much slower speeds? Because that's the only feasible way to play the game without flying off the road or off a 500 foot canyon without warning.

Now, I drive for a living (which is how I got as much enjoyment from this game as I have) so my first reaction to this new change is to say "well... maybe put some speed limit signs on the road to indicate when the player can speed up or needs to slow down. But the premise of the game is that its post-apocalyptic. So that might be too much to ask. In terms of gameplay, if the devs are planning on keeping the current world generation, the total distance needs to be cut down by A LOT.  You have got to be out of your mind to think anyone is going to tickle the brake and accelerator, making sure to stay under 70 kph, for 5000 kilometres while dodging those wide rock formations that now spawn in the road. On top of already needing to stop and figure out which road has the light poles.

I don't even have any complaints about the survival elements. As a matter of fact, food and water could even be more scarce to find. The key elements from the current public version I liked was being able to dump tracks onto the custom radio, manage gas (which also isn't that hard) piece together the Plymouth and just floor it down the road. Needing just enough attention to dodge the rocks and poles. The main source of difficulty already comes from the 5000 km distance.

I haven't even experienced the rest of what was added or changed in the test beta. The road generation was already too much to handle after flying a hundred feet off of cliffs way too often. Again, I know there are other things planned in development, but the current test beta is no indication of good change in the future, It might sound like lip service but I believe this game has potential not just to be some shitty streamer meme game. I'd rather play the current public version than some trucking sim or whatever driving iteration of Microsoft's flight sim.

The game is fun if you understand what you're getting yourself into. This isn't a serious driving game, or a game with really advanced mechanics. It is full of jank, and that can be part of the fun if you can endure the jank being the cause of the end of a play through.

Most of the jank comes from the physics of the game which just go absolutely insane from time to time for virtually no reason. The road generation can lead to issues where the road disappears into a mountain, or the road goes off a steep cliff, or a vertical incline. Points of interest are sometimes inaccessible thanks to the map generation. 

There are some basic car maintenance components, such as oil, fuel, and water for your radiator. There are also survival aspects with food and water, as well as enemy NPCs to deal with. There is no wear and tear or tires or car parts. You can also swap out tires, engines, etc. in most vehicles. There is even a 2 stroke engine which requires you to know how much oil/gas you put in it to run properly. 

There was a beta released quite some time ago that featured another vehicle and some map and road generation changes. Sadly, none of that ever got updated to be an actual release of the game, and the game has not been updated in forever. This developer seems to have moved onto another project (Horizon anyone?) or projects. That's all fine and good, but take the game out of "early development" and just retire it and state this is the game, take it as is. 

It is a shame this game was abandoned, because as I said, it can be a relaxing bit of good fun. You can be as serious or as goofy with it as you like. I just with there was more done with it.

The game:
Very fun and weird game with some surprising mechanics (e.g. you can drink from cacti). You've probably seen videos with the weirdness and jank, but to me that's a feature. The car customization is just the right level of complexity. You can play without enemies and hunger/thirst, if you want a relaxing experience. The last beta added online coop (multiplayer) but it's janky like the rest of the game.

The situation:
No updates in over two years (latest beta was in Nov 2024). The dev made a meme post in July 2025 that the game is not abandoned, but since then nothing has followed, so I do not believe that until the game gets an actual update. (AFAIK the game is a one-man project so I guess the dev got burned out since updates used to be frequent)
There's still huge potential here, because a lot of things need to be fixed (bugs and foremost performance!) and basic things like achievements and save file sync (Steam Cloud) are missing. If the game ever gets workshop support, the possibilities are endless.

Verdict:
Buy on deep sale only, unless the dev shows up again.

Not Recommended – A Case Study in How Not to Make a Game

The Long Drive isn’t a game. It’s a collection of unfinished ideas held together by broken physics and blind optimism. Calling this an “experience” feels like an excuse rather than a description.

The core gameplay loop is empty to the point of absurdity. You drive through nothing, for no meaningful reason, while fighting mechanics that seem actively hostile to the player. The physics don’t feel quirky or experimental — they feel untested. Objects glitch, vehicles behave like they’re possessed, and basic interactions collapse under their own incompetence.

Bugs aren’t occasional here; they’re the foundation. Performance issues, random crashes, physics explosions, and nonsensical behavior are treated as features instead of failures. It gives the impression that nobody ever stopped to ask the most important question in game development: “Is this actually fun?”

The worst part is how much this game relies on excuses. “It’s supposed to be janky.” “It’s part of the charm.” No — jank isn’t charm when it replaces design, balance, and polish. Randomness is not depth. Frustration is not difficulty. And emptiness is not atmosphere.

There’s no sense of progression, no satisfying mechanics, and no reward for sticking with it. Just an endless road that mirrors the development philosophy: long, directionless, and painfully hollow.

This feels less like a passion project and more like a reminder that having access to a game engine does not mean you should use it. Early Access is not a shield against criticism, and “experimental” is not a synonym for unfinished.

Conclusion:
The Long Drive is a waste of time, patience, and potential. If this game represents the developer’s vision, then the most merciful outcome would be to stop, reflect, and reconsider whether game development is the right medium at all.

To preface this, I love this game and want it to succeed. Sadly the developer does not share my opinion. They kept adding things to the game that nobody really asked for, while neglecting to fix/develop things that people want fixed or further developed. And now it seems the game is basically abandoned.

Here are some issues that were never resolved in no particular order:
1. The driving physics are all over the place. 
2. Some cars just spin out of control for no reason when you go above 100km/h.
3. Your car gets stuck on nothing when you crash, when you roll or even when you just drive normally.
4. Objects can get stuck on other objects and you can't pull them apart. They can also phase out of existence or fall through the floor.
5. Opening a door the wrong way can kill you.
6. Some cars can't climb a slight incline without overheating.
7. The player character's arms and hands look deformed when performing any kind of action.
8. The player character's model and animations look unfinished.
9. Picking up a weapon or usable item and using them takes too many steps.
10. The buildings and other points of interest you can discover are not particularly interesting and repeat too often.
11. The abandoned cars you come across often have the wrong fluids inside of their tanks, and if you didn't have the foresight to get the hose in the beginning, you can't drain them in order to replace those fluids, rendering most cars you find useless unless you go all the way back to the starting location to get the hose.
12. Grass is too unoptimized and causes stutters even on higher end PCs.
Just to name a few.

And I know what some of the excuses are gonna be. "Don't be so critical man, it's just one guy who's learning as he's developing it." I don't care. You're being charged money for the game, you have a right to expect a functioning game. If he really has so much trouble fixing the game, he should put the source code up on GitHub and have the community contribute fixes. There's really no excuse at this point.

Game has serious marketing lies issue, if you read the game page it's making it sounds that replayability and roguelike mode are features that give extra values that you would get from playing this game like with so many other non linear story driven games out there...

It turns out that's not the case in ways that's worse than not having those features... Long story short the game actually forces you to replay the game over and over again and use the roguelike system, but they somehow locked the roguelike benefits behind the hardest game difficulty onky...

I've replayed the game like half a dozen times before giving up, and I played on normal difficulty mode, which should be reasonable for all games, the problem is that :

1. The game constantly throws a insurmountable walls at somewhat consistent intervals at you via it's combat, they give you enemies you virtually can't even beat even via save scumming (unless you want to replay the combat 1000x or something to make sure every single turn based randomness goes you ways)...

So for example you have learned to play the combat after the first or 2nd combat in the game, but then at the 3rd combat suddenly it became clear that you couldn't win, so you have to restart the game to pick better story branches or to better prepare yourself to win that 3rd combat, but then later on in the 6th combat the game would throw the exact same thing at you again, and it goes on like that in the future as well... 

2. Without the actual roguelike mechanics that gives your character meta progression (which is locked behind the game hardest difficulty), you're practically given worse experience at looping the game, you'd feel frustration because the lack of crucial player agency to progress further.

3. And the worst of it is that this is supposedly a game that proud itself in the narration side of the game as well with game changing decisions etc, so you're being forced to kill your immersion there because you're being chained by "roguelike progression design" that force you to relive your decisions over and over or to pick different story choices just to see what would happen that could release you from your frustration at hitting the frequent insurmountable walls and throwing your role playing immersion out of the window altogether... This is my biggest issue with this game... I thought the game would be more of an non linear adventure than roguelike.
The wolves when you scavenge are more dangerous than your first battle against human.

I wanted to like this game. I tried really hard to like this game. But it keeps being an annoyance. Some examples.

Mandatory quest, I couldn't avoid it, it was required of me. This resulted in losing points off the max of a stat, or failing the quest spectacularly...which you only find out after being through many steps that take quite a while. That's not a hard choice, that's a stupid choice. Not losing points off the current value of the stat, which can fluctuate and be rebuilt, points off the MAX, and there doesn't seem to be anything bringing that up, it either stays the same or goes down, so it's permanently damaging my character to succeed with the mandatory quest.

Combat is slow as watching paint dry. So slow. Games like this generally offer a 2x 3x 4x option for combat speed. Not this one. Even so, it's coded in such a way that it'll still have some stutters during combat for at least some people (based on your cpu, and mine is quite good so idk what the problem is).

It has some good elements, but it's a lot of finding out what you need then save scumming if you want things to be done in a timely manner that leaves you enough time to train and develop your character. Like, a quest requires doing certain things, but who knows where the stuff is or how to craft the thing, it's a definite learning process. Once you learn it, no big deal and you can zip right through, but until then you'll waste a lot of time (the measured resource in the game, not just irl time, you only have so much between major events). So, save scum, or fail a lot until you die and restart and zip through what you previously played through.

I'm just regularly annoyed. Setting it down for the moment. Maybe I'll try to continue later or tomorrow, I've got the basics down and...ugh, sitting at the point of that quest that punishes me for success. So annoying.

I will change this review once I deem the game to have been properly fixed, but as of now it runs into several severe errors whenever you go through certain routes or use different items. The current major issue is in the "Threads of Destiny" DLC, but this applies to the overall state of the game. In the DLC, you have a chance to encounter a mounted knight and challenge him. Assuming you save every single time you do anything, you'll be fine probably, but other wise you are hard locked with the game constantly crashing upon entrance to the fight, with it saying "Could not find image" for the knight. Now if they had say an auto save section where it saves before actions, like most VN's, it wouldn't be an issue, rollback and just don't challenge. However; they do not, so if you do this challenge, and haven't saved recently or saved right before the fight, congrats you gotta restart. A shame since it is a fun game, but I do not recommend anyone purchase it at this moment, especially not at full price.
